## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define plethysmography. List the two main types studied in this module.**

**Model Answer:**
Plethysmography is the technique of measuring volume changes of a body segment or organ due to circulatory (pulsatile blood) activity. The two principal types in the KTU 2024 syllabus are **(i) Photoelectric Plethysmograph (PPG)**, which senses changes in light absorption, and **(ii) Impedance Plethysmograph (IPG)**, which senses changes in electrical impedance of a body segment.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. State Fick's principle. Write the equation for cardiac output using this principle.**

**Model Answer:**
Fick's principle states that the rate of uptake of a substance by an organ equals the blood flow through the organ multiplied by the arterio-venous concentration difference of that substance. Applied to oxygen uptake in the lungs:

$$\text{CO} = \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{[O_2]_{\text{arterial}} - [O_2]_{\text{venous}}}$$

where $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ is the oxygen consumption (mL O₂/min) and the concentrations are in mL O₂/L blood. The result is in L/min.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU Model Question**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2, Set A]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **continuous-wave ultrasonic blood flowmeter**. Derive the Doppler shift equation $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_t v \cos\theta}{c}$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** In a Doppler experiment, the transmitted frequency is 8 MHz, the angle between the beam and flow direction is 45°, and the recorded Doppler shift is 800 Hz. Find (i) the blood velocity, and (ii) the volume flow if the vessel diameter is 6 mm. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s in tissue.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working of CW Ultrasonic Blood Flowmeter:**

- Two piezo-electric crystals are mounted in a probe — one acts as a transmitter, the other as a receiver.
- The transmitter is excited by a high-frequency oscillator (typically 2–10 MHz) producing a continuous ultrasonic beam.
- The beam is directed through the skin into the blood vessel at an angle $\theta$ to the direction of flow.
- Moving red blood cells act as scatterers; the back-scattered ultrasound experiences a **Doppler frequency shift** $\Delta f$.
- The receiving crystal picks up the shifted frequency; the two signals (transmitted and received) are fed to a **mixer/multiplier**.
- The mixer output contains a difference frequency $\Delta f$ (in the audible range) — this is the Doppler-shifted signal.
- This is amplified and displayed (audibly as a "whooshing" sound and as a velocity waveform).

**Derivation of Doppler shift** (refer to derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes):

$$\Delta f = \frac{2 f_t v \cos\theta}{c} \quad \blacksquare$$

where $f_t$ = transmitted frequency, $v$ = velocity of RBC, $\theta$ = angle between beam and flow, $c$ = velocity of sound in tissue.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $f_t = 8 \times 10^6$ Hz, $\theta = 45°$ → $\cos 45° = 0.7071$, $\Delta f = 800$ Hz, $D = 6$ mm = $6 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $c = 1540$ m/s.

**(i) Velocity:**

$$v = \frac{c \cdot \Delta f}{2 f_t \cos\theta} = \frac{1540 \times 800}{2 \times 8 \times 10^6 \times 0.7071}$$

$$v = \frac{1.232 \times 10^6}{1.1314 \times 10^7} = 0.1089\ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 10.9\ \text{cm/s}}$$

**(ii) Volume flow:**

Area $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (6 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 36 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2$

$$Q = A \cdot v = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.1089 = 3.08 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Converting to mL/min: $1\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} = 10^6\ \text{mL} \times 60 = 6 \times 10^7\ \text{mL/min}$.

$$Q = 3.08 \times 10^{-6} \times 6 \times 10^7 = 184.7\ \text{mL/min}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 185\ \text{mL/min}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly (i) forget to use $\cos\theta$ (setting it to 1) and (ii) mismatch units between vessel diameter (mm vs. m) and time (s vs. min). In KTU answer sheets, **always write the units explicitly** at every step.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a **photoelectric plethysmograph (PPG)**. State Beer–Lambert's law and explain how PPG signals are derived from it.

**(b) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **impedance plethysmograph (IPG)**. A forearm segment of length 25 cm has a baseline impedance of 60 Ω. The pulsatile change is 0.15 Ω. Given $\rho = 150\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}$, calculate the pulse volume change. State the medical significance of this measurement.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Photoelectric Plethysmograph:**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.3):** LED driver → red (660 nm) and IR (940 nm) LEDs → finger/earlobe tissue → photodiode → transimpedance amplifier → DC reject → AC amplifier → PPG output.

**Working Principle:**
- LEDs emit light that passes through (or is reflected from) the tissue.
- Light is absorbed by skin, bone, venous blood, and arterial blood.
- The arterial blood volume pulsates with each heartbeat, modulating the transmitted/reflected light intensity.
- The photodetector converts the optical signal into an electrical one.
- The signal has a **DC offset** (steady tissue absorption) and a small **AC pulsatile component** (due to arterial blood volume swing).
- High-pass filtering extracts the AC component → the **plethysmogram**.

**Beer–Lambert Law:**

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon c d}$$

where $I_0$ = incident intensity, $I$ = transmitted intensity, $\epsilon$ = extinction coefficient, $c$ = concentration, $d$ = path length.

In PPG, the optical path length *d* (or concentration *c* in the case of varying blood volume) modulates $I$. A small change $\Delta v$ in blood volume produces:

$$\frac{\Delta I}{I_0} \approx -k \cdot \Delta v$$

Thus the PPG waveform is a faithful reproduction of the pulsatile arterial volume.

**(b) Impedance Plethysmograph:**

**Block Diagram (refer Section 4.4):** High-frequency oscillator (50–100 kHz) → constant current source (1–5 mA) → 4 electrodes (2 outer injecting, 2 inner sensing) → differential amplifier → phase-sensitive detector → DC stripper → $\Delta Z(t)$ output.

**Working Principle:**
- A small sub-sensory high-frequency AC current is passed through the outer electrodes.
- Voltage is sensed by the inner electrodes; the ratio $V/I$ gives the impedance of the limb segment.
- The impedance has a steady DC component $Z_0$ and a tiny AC pulsatile component $\Delta Z$.
- $\Delta Z$ reflects the pulsatile change in blood volume.
- The **Nyboer formula** converts $\Delta Z$ into $\Delta V$ (pulse volume change).

**Numerical Solution:**

Given: $L = 25$ cm, $Z_0 = 60\ \Omega$, $\Delta Z = 0.15\ \Omega$, $\rho = 150\ \Omega\!\cdot\!\text{cm}$.

$$\Delta V = -\frac{\rho L^2}{Z_0^2} \cdot \Delta Z = -\frac{150 \times 25^2}{60^2} \times 0.15$$

$$\Delta V = -\frac{150 \times 625}{3600} \times 0.15 = -\frac{93750}{3600} \times 0.15 = -26.04 \times 0.15 = -3.91\ \text{mL}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \Delta V \vert \approx 3.91\ \text{mL per heartbeat}}$$

**Medical Significance:**
- The pulse volume change reflects **arterial inflow** per heartbeat in the limb segment.
- Useful in assessing **peripheral vascular disease (PVD)**, deep vein thrombosis (DVT), and venous occlusion.
- Reduced $\Delta V$ indicates arterial insufficiency; prolonged $\Delta V$ recovery after cuff occlusion is diagnostic of venous obstruction.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — IPG:** (i) Students often drop the **negative sign** in Nyboer's equation — KTU strictly penalizes sign errors. (ii) Confusing $\Delta Z$ (in Ω) with $\Delta V$ (in mL) costs 1–2 marks. (iii) The 4-electrode (tetrapolar) configuration must be mentioned; the 2-electrode version is a common wrong answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memory Points for KTU 2024 Module 2:**

- ☐ **Plethysmography** measures **volume changes** of body parts; two KTU-required types are **Photoelectric (PPG)** and **Impedance (IPG)**.
- ☐ **EM flowmeter** uses Faraday's law: $E = B D \bar{v}$ — needs **surgical exposure**; uses **AC excitation** to prevent electrode polarization.
- ☐ **Doppler flowmeter** uses $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_t v \cos\theta}{c}$; CW uses two crystals; Pulsed Doppler is range-gated.
- ☐ **Fick principle** is the gold standard: $\text{CO} = \dot{V}_{O_2} / ([O_2]_a - [O_2]_v)$.
- ☐ **Stewart–Hamilton** indicator-dilution: $\text{CO} = m \big/ \int_0^\infty C(t) dt$ — needs **recirculation correction**.
- ☐ **PPG** uses Beer–Lambert: $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\epsilon c d}$; pulsatile AC / DC ratio = **perfusion index**.
- ☐ **IPG** uses **4 electrodes** (tetrapolar), **50–100 kHz, 1–5 mA**; **Nyboer formula:** $\Delta V = -\rho L^2 \Delta Z / Z_0^2$.
- ☐ Sign convention: **volume ↑ → impedance ↓** (negative sign in Nyboer).
- ☐ PPG sites: finger, earlobe, toe, forehead (reflectance mode for forehead).
- ☐ Speed of sound in soft tissue $c \approx \mathbf{1540\ m/s}$.
- ☐ Typical Doppler beam angle θ ≈ **30°–60°**; at θ = 90°, no signal.
- ☐ Normal **resting CO ≈ 5 L/min**; **cardiac index ≈ 2.5–4.0 L/min/m²**.
- ☐ IPG clinical uses: PVD screening, **DVT detection**, venous occlusion plethysmography.
- ☐ PPG dual-wavelength (660 + 940 nm) enables **pulse oximetry (SpO₂)** — frequently asked as a follow-up.
- ☐ The **dye** used in indicator dilution is **indocyanine green (ICG)** because it is non-toxic and rapidly cleared.
- ☐ **Exponential downstroke extrapolation** is required to remove recirculation artifact in dye-dilution curves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** If you remember the **three master equations** — Faraday ($E = BD\bar{v}$), Doppler ($\Delta f = 2 f_t v \cos\theta / c$), and Nyboer ($\Delta V = -\rho L^2 \Delta Z / Z_0^2$) — along with **Fick and Stewart–Hamilton** for CO, you can answer ~85% of Module 2 numerical and Part B questions in the KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Heart Sounds (Phonocardiography)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT611 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Heart Sounds (Phonocardiography)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phonocardiography (PCG)** is the non-invasive, graphical recording of the acoustic vibrations produced by the mechanical activity of the heart (valvular closures, blood flow turbulence, and myocardial vibrations) displayed as amplitude-versus-time traces called **Phonocardiograms**.

The instrument used to perform this measurement is called a **Phonocardiograph**, and the transducer that converts the acoustic energy of heart sounds into a corresponding electrical signal is known as a **Heart Sound Transducer** or **Cardiac Microphone**.

### Normal Heart Sounds (Standard Clinical Classification)

| Sound | Mechanical Origin | Frequency Range | Duration | Timing in Cardiac Cycle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S₁ ("Lub")** | Closure of Mitral (M1) & Tricuspid (T1) valves | **25 – 45 Hz** | ~0.14 s | Start of systole |
| **S₂ ("Dub")** | Closure of Aortic (A2) & Pulmonary (P2) valves | **50 – 80 Hz** | ~0.11 s | End of systole |
| **S₃ (Third Sound)** | Rapid ventricular filling in early diastole | **25 – 35 Hz** | ~0.04 s | Early diastole |
| **S₄ (Fourth Sound)** | Atrial contraction forcing blood into stiff ventricle | **15 – 30 Hz** | ~0.04 s | Late diastole (Pre-systole) |
| **Murmurs** | Turbulent blood flow across stenotic/incompetent valves or septal defects | **100 – 1000 Hz** | Variable | Systolic / Diastolic |

> [!NOTE]
> **Auscultation vs. Phonocardiography:** *Auscultation* (using a stethoscope) is qualitative listening. *Phonocardiography* is quantitative, objective, time-correlated recording that enables frequency, intensity, duration, and timing analysis — essential for permanent documentation and computer-aided diagnosis.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **The "Tuning-Into-Heart" Analogy:** Imagine pressing your ear against a thin wall and hearing muffled voices from the next room. Your ear drum vibrates faintly. A **stethoscope** simply amplifies this passive listening — like cupping your hand behind your ear. Now, imagine a journalist placing a **high-fidelity microphone** on that wall, connecting it to a **recorder** that draws a graph of every word's loudness over time. That recorded graph is exactly what a **Phonocardiograph** does for the heart. The microphone (transducer) converts heart's mechanical "whispers" (≈20–2000 Hz, extremely low intensity) into a clean, measurable, drawable electrical signal — a *phonocardiogram* — much like a seismograph records earthquakes.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Reference threshold of human hearing:** $P_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ Pa$
- **Speed of sound in body tissue:** $c \approx 1540 \ m/s$
- **Acoustic impedance of soft tissue:** $Z \approx 1.63 \times 10^6 \ Rayl$
- **Heart sound intensity (S1, S2) at chest wall:** typically **45 – 60 dB SPL** above threshold
- **Diaphragm displacement during heart sounds:** on the order of **nanometers to micrometers**

---

## 1.3 Why Phonocardiography is Clinically Significant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** While echocardiography and ECG dominate modern cardiology, **PCG remains a low-cost, non-invasive, radiation-free screening tool** that excels at detecting:
> - **Valvular heart diseases** (mitral stenosis, aortic regurgitation)
> - **Congenital septal defects** (VSD, ASD via characteristic murmurs)
> - **Pericardial friction rubs** (pericarditis)
> - **Prosthetic valve dysfunction** (abnormal clicking, murmurs)
>
> Modern **digital PCG systems** integrated with **machine-learning classifiers** are now emerging as AI-driven cardiac screening tools, especially in **telemedicine and rural KTU-affiliated health camps** in Kerala.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of a Phonocardiograph

A modern PCG system consists of the following signal-chain blocks, each performing a specific function:

1. **Heart-Sound Source (Coupling Medium):** The thoracic wall acts as a low-pass mechanical filter, attenuating high-frequency components above ~600 Hz before sound reaches the transducer.
2. **Transducer (Cardiac Microphone):** Converts acoustic pressure waves into an analog electrical signal. The choice of transducer governs bandwidth, sensitivity, and noise performance.
3. **Preamplifier:** Boosts the very weak transducer output (typically in the **microvolt to millivolt** range) using a low-noise instrumentation front-end.
4. **Band-Pass Filter Bank (Spectral Splitter):** Separates the PCG signal into multiple bands so that low-frequency S3/S4 components are not swamped by higher-frequency S1/S2/murmur components.
5. **Main Amplifier & Gain Control:** Provides variable gain (typically **40 – 100 dB**) to bring the signal to ADC input range.
6. **Display / Recording Module:** Analog → thermal-paper strip-chart recorder (legacy) or digital → LCD/PC display.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why a preamp?** Heart-sound transducers produce signals as low as **10 μV** — far below ADC quantization thresholds.
- **Why band-pass filters?** Heart sounds span ~20 Hz to ~2000 Hz. A **single broadband channel** mixes murmurs with low-frequency S3/S4, reducing diagnostic clarity. Filtering isolates diagnostically relevant bands.
- **Why mechanical coupling matters?** Air gaps between sensor and skin reflect most acoustic energy (impedance mismatch). A **gel or air-cushion coupling** is mandatory to maximize transmission.

---

## 2.2 Types of Cardiac Microphones (Transducers)

### 2.2.1 Piezoelectric Microphone (Most Common in PCG)

- **Working Principle:** Uses the *direct piezoelectric effect* — certain crystals (e.g., **lead zirconate titanate, PZT**, or **PVDF polymer**) generate a charge $Q$ proportional to applied mechanical stress $\sigma$:
$$Q = d \cdot \sigma \cdot A$$

  where $d$ is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient** (C/N) and $A$ is the electrode area (m²).
- **Advantages:** High sensitivity, wide frequency response (**0.1 Hz to >10 kHz**), no external power needed, rugged.
- **Disadvantages:** Temperature sensitive, output impedance very high → requires **FET-input preamplifier**.

### 2.2.2 Condenser (Capacitive) Microphone

- **Working Principle:** A thin metallized diaphragm forms one plate of a parallel-plate capacitor. Sound pressure changes the plate separation $x$, changing capacitance:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{x}$$
- A small change $\Delta x$ produces $\Delta C$, which is converted to voltage via a **charge preamplifier** or **electret biasing**.

### 2.2.3 Electret Microphone

- A permanently polarized dielectric (electret) replaces the external bias voltage, making it **small, cheap, and battery-free** — the modern choice for **electronic stethoscopes**.

### 2.2.4 Dynamic (Moving-Coil) Microphone

- A coil attached to the diaphragm moves in a magnetic field, generating EMF by Faraday's law: $e = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$. Robust but **bulky and limited high-frequency response**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the core of any KTU numerical on PCG microphones and signal levels.**

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | $P$ = RMS sound pressure; $P_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa$ | **Decibels (dB SPL)** |
| 2 | Reference Threshold | $P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ | Threshold of human hearing | Pa |
| 3 | Capacitance of Condenser Mic | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{x}$ | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $A$ = plate area; $x$ = separation | Farads (F) |
| 4 | Sensitivity (Microphone) | $S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | Open-circuit output per unit pressure | V/Pa |
| 5 | Piezoelectric Charge | $Q = d \cdot \sigma \cdot A$ | $d$ = charge coefficient; $\sigma$ = stress | Coulombs (C) |
| 6 | Open-Circuit Voltage (Piezo) | $V_{oc} = \dfrac{d \cdot \sigma \cdot t}{\varepsilon}$ | $t$ = crystal thickness; $\varepsilon$ = permittivity | Volts (V) |
| 7 | Resonant Frequency (Crystal) | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus; $\rho$ = density; $t$ = thickness | Hz |
| 8 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \cdot c$ | $\rho$ = tissue density; $c$ = speed of sound | Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹) |
| 9 | Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | dB |
| 10 | Heart-Sound Frequency Band | $f_{S1}, f_{S2}, f_{S3}, f_{S4}$ | See Section 1.1 table | **25–80 Hz** (S1,S2) **15–35 Hz** (S3,S4) |
| 11 | Murmur Frequency Band | $f_{murmur}$ | Turbulent flow spectrum | **100 – 1000 Hz** |
| 12 | SNR of PCG Front-End | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Diagnostic clarity metric | dB |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronic Stethoscopes (e.g., 3M Littmann CORE):** Apply PCG front-ends with active noise cancellation.
- **Fetal Heart Sound Monitoring:** PCG is used in **intrapartum monitoring** where ECG is not feasible.
- **AI-Based Cardiac Screening (CNN-PCG Models):** PCG recordings now feed deep-learning models (e.g., **Potes et al., 2016 PhysioNet Challenge**) that classify normal/abnormal heart sounds with >85% accuracy.
- **Biomedical R&D (KTU Final-Year Projects):** Many KTU B.Tech projects use **Arduino/STM32 + electret mic + Python DSP** for low-cost PCG devices targeted at rural Kerala primary health centres.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) of a Heart Sound

### Problem Statement
A normal S1 heart sound produces an RMS sound pressure of **0.05 Pa** at the chest wall. Calculate its Sound Pressure Level in dB SPL. State whether this level is audible against a quiet-room background of **30 dB SPL**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of SPL in decibels:**

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0}\right) \ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the given quantities:**

$P_{rms} = 0.05 \ \text{Pa}$ (sound pressure of S1)

$P_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \ \text{Pa}$ (reference threshold)

**Step 3 — Form the pressure ratio:**

$$\frac{P_{rms}}{P_0} = \frac{0.05}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 2500$$

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm:**

$$\log_{10}(2500) = \log_{10}(2.5 \times 10^3) = \log_{10}(2.5) + 3 = 0.3979 + 3 = 3.3979$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by 20:**

$$L_p = 20 \times 3.3979 = 67.958 \ \text{dB SPL}$$

**Step 6 — Final boxed result with units:**

$$\boxed{L_p \approx 68 \ \text{dB SPL}}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with the quiet-room background (30 dB SPL):**

$$\Delta L = 68 - 30 = 38 \ \text{dB}$$

Since the signal is **38 dB above the ambient noise floor**, it is **clearly audible** and well within the linear operating range of a typical PCG preamplifier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 38 dB difference corresponds to a **pressure ratio of ~80×**, meaning the heart sound is 80 times louder in pressure amplitude than the ambient noise. This ensures a healthy **SNR > 35 dB**, the clinical benchmark for diagnostic-grade PCG recordings.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sensitivity of a Condenser Microphone

### Problem Statement
A condenser microphone has a diaphragm of effective area $A = 25 \ \text{mm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^2$, plate separation $x_0 = 20 \ \mu m = 20 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}$, and is connected to a charge preamplifier of feedback capacitance $C_f = 100 \ \text{pF}$. The diaphragm displacement under normal S1 sound is $\Delta x = 1 \ \text{nm} = 10^{-9} \ \text{m}$. Determine:
1. The static capacitance $C_0$ of the microphone.
2. The change in capacitance $\Delta C$ caused by S1.
3. The output voltage $V_{out}$ of the charge preamplifier (assume $C_0 \gg C_f$ justifies the ideal gain expression $V_{out} \approx \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C_f}$, where $\Delta Q$ is the charge imbalance created by the capacitance change at a constant bias $V_b = 50 \ \text{V}$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Static capacitance from parallel-plate formula:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x_0} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(25 \times 10^{-6})}{20 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-16}}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.1068 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{F} \approx 11.07 \ \text{pF}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate $C$ with respect to $x$ to obtain $\Delta C$:**

Starting from $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x}$, differentiate:

$$\frac{dC}{dx} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{x^2} = -\frac{C_0}{x_0}$$

**Step 3 — Apply small-displacement approximation:**

$$\Delta C \approx \left(\frac{dC}{dx}\right) \Delta x = -\frac{C_0}{x_0} \Delta x$$

$$\Delta C = -\frac{(11.07 \times 10^{-12})(10^{-9})}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = -\frac{1.107 \times 10^{-20}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\Delta C = -5.535 \times 10^{-16} \ \text{F} \approx -0.554 \ \text{fF}$$

The **negative sign** indicates that an *increase* in plate separation *decreases* capacitance — a crucial polarity detail in PCG front-end design.

**Step 4 — Charge imbalance from constant bias $V_b$:**

Initial charge: $Q_0 = C_0 V_b = (11.07 \times 10^{-12})(50) = 5.535 \times 10^{-10} \ \text{C}$

Charge change: $\Delta Q = \Delta C \cdot V_b = (-5.535 \times 10^{-16})(50) = -2.767 \times 10^{-14} \ \text{C}$

**Step 5 — Charge preamplifier output voltage:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{\Delta Q}{C_f} = \frac{-2.767 \times 10^{-14}}{100 \times 10^{-12}} = -2.767 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx -276.7 \ \mu V}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The output of the condenser microphone is on the order of **hundreds of microvolts** — this is why every PCG must begin with a **low-noise instrumentation preamplifier** (e.g., INA128, AD620) with a **noise floor below 1 μV RMS** to preserve diagnostic fidelity. A 276 μV signal can be comfortably amplified by 60 dB to a 276 mV display signal.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Resonant Frequency of a Piezoelectric PCG Crystal

### Problem Statement
A PZT crystal of thickness $t = 2 \ \text{mm}$, Young's modulus $E = 60 \ \text{GPa}$, and density $\rho = 7500 \ \text{kg/m}^3$ is used as a PCG transducer. Calculate its longitudinal resonant frequency $f_r$ and state whether it is suitable for capturing murmurs up to 1000 Hz.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Use the longitudinal resonance formula:**

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2t}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the speed of sound in the crystal:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}} = \sqrt{\frac{60 \times 10^9}{7500}} = \sqrt{8 \times 10^6} = 2828.43 \ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the resonance equation:**

$$f_r = \frac{2828.43}{2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2828.43}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.07 \times 10^5 \ \text{Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_r \approx 707 \ \text{kHz}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

Since $f_r \approx 707 \ \text{kHz} \gg 1000 \ \text{Hz}$, the crystal operates in its **flat, non-resonant (stiffness-controlled) region** within the entire heart-sound band. This guarantees a **uniform frequency response** from DC up to roughly $f_r / 10 \approx 70 \ \text{kHz}$ — far more bandwidth than any PCG application requires. ✅ **The crystal is therefore well-suited for full-bandwidth PCG, including murmurs.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Transducer Selection for PCG

| Parameter | Piezoelectric (PZT) | Electret Condenser | Dynamic (Moving Coil) | Carbon Microphone (Legacy) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Response | 0.1 Hz – 10 kHz ✅ | 20 Hz – 20 kHz ✅ | 50 Hz – 15 kHz ⚠ | 200 Hz – 4 kHz ❌ |
| Sensitivity | High ✅ | High ✅ | Medium ⚠ | Low ❌ |
| Output Impedance | Very High → FET preamp needed | High → Preamp needed | Low → Direct coupling ✅ | High |
| External Power | Not required ✅ | Not required ✅ | Not required ✅ | Required ❌ |
| Size / Wearability | Small ✅ | Very small ✅ | Bulky ❌ | Small ✅ |
| Cost | Medium | Low ✅ | High | Low |
| Diagnostic Use | Standard clinical PCG ✅ | Electronic stethoscopes ✅ | Rarely used | Obsolete ❌ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently asked transducer question in KTU 2024 PCG topics is: *"Justify why piezoelectric transducers are preferred for PCG over dynamic microphones."* The answer revolves around the **wider bandwidth (covering murmurs up to 1 kHz)** and **higher sensitivity to low-frequency vibrations** (S3, S4, and mid-diastolic murmurs) — areas where dynamic microphones roll off.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Phonocardiograph

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Heart Sound Source] --> A2[Thoracic Wall Filter]
    A2 --> A3[Acoustic Coupling Gel]
    A3 --> B1[Cardiac Microphone Transducer]
    B1 --> C1[Pre Amplifier Low Noise]
    C1 --> D1[Band Pass Filter 20 to 2000 Hz]
    D1 --> E1[Main Amplifier Variable Gain]
    E1 --> F1[Signal Conditioner Rectifier and Smoother]
    F1 --> G1[Display Module Oscilloscope or PC]
    G1 --> H1[Storage and Diagnostic Output]

    D1 -.-> D2[Low Pass Channel for S3 S4]
    D1 -.-> D3[Mid Band Channel for S1 S2]
    D1 -.-> D4[High Pass Channel for Murmurs]

    B1 -.-> BX1[Piezoelectric or Electret or Condenser]
    C1 -.-> CX1[INA128 Instrumentation Front End]

    style A1 fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#0e7490,color:#ffffff
    style D1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style G1 fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style H1 fill:#166534,color:#ffffff
    style D2 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style D3 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style D4 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
```

### Description of the Block Diagram

The signal flow proceeds left-to-right:

1. **Heart → Thoracic Wall → Coupling Gel:** Mechanical vibrations propagate through chest tissue (low-pass mechanical filter) and reach the diaphragm via an acoustic-impedance-matching medium.
2. **Transducer:** Converts acoustic pressure into an electrical voltage. The dashed annotation lists the three practical transducer options.
3. **Preamplifier:** A low-noise, high-CMRR front-end (e.g., INA128) amplifies the μV-level signal to a workable mV level.
4. **Band-Pass Filter Bank:** Splits the signal into three diagnostic channels — low, mid, and high — to isolate S3/S4, S1/S2, and murmurs respectively.
5. **Main Amplifier:** Provides user-adjustable gain (40–100 dB).
6. **Signal Conditioner:** Performs envelope detection, rectification, and smoothing for amplitude-based displays.
7. **Display / Storage:** Real-time visualization on oscilloscope or PC, with archival storage for later analysis.

---

## 4.2 Phono Timing Diagram (PCG vs. ECG)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Diastole] --> P1[P Wave]
    P1 --> P2[QRS Complex]
    P2 --> P3[S1 Heart Sound]
    P3 --> P4[ST Segment]
    P4 --> P5[T Wave]
    P5 --> P6[S2 Heart Sound]
    P6 --> P7[S3 Optional]
    P7 --> P8[Diastolic Pause]
    P8 --> P9[S4 Optional Presystolic]
    P9 --> P0
    P3 -.-> P3A[Mitral and Tricuspid Closure 25 to 45 Hz]
    P6 -.-> P6A[Aortic and Pulmonary Closure 50 to 80 Hz]
    P7 -.-> P7A[Ventricular Filling 25 to 35 Hz]
    P9 -.-> P9A[Atrial Kick 15 to 30 Hz]

    style P0 fill:#1f2937,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style P6 fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff
    style P7 fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style P9 fill:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    style P3A fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style P6A fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style P7A fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
    style P9A fill:#fef3c7,color:#000000
```

### Description of the Timing Diagram

This diagram correlates the **electrocardiogram (ECG) electrical events** (P, QRS, T) with the **phonocardiogram mechanical events** (S1, S2, S3, S4). Students must memorize that:

- **S1 immediately follows the QRS complex** (since electrical depolarization triggers ventricular contraction and mitral/tricuspid closure).
- **S2 immediately follows the T wave** (repolarization completes ventricular ejection, snapping the aortic and pulmonary valves shut).
- **S3 occurs in early diastole** (passive ventricular filling).
- **S4 occurs in late diastole, just before the next P wave** (atrial contraction).

This timing relationship is the cornerstone of **synchronizing PCG with ECG** in any modern polygraph or Holter-type cardiac recorder.

---

## 4.3 Transducer Coupling Methods — Block Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Sensor to Skin Interface] --> K2[Direct Contact Coupling]
    K1 --> K3[Air Cushion Coupling]
    K1 --> K4[Acoustic Gel Coupling]
    K2 --> K2A[Bell type stethoscope chestpiece minimal air gap best for low frequencies]
    K3 --> K3A[Diaphragm type stethoscope controlled air chamber best for mid to high frequencies]
    K4 --> K4A[Ultrasound gel impedance matching broadband research grade setup]

    style K1 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
    style K2 fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style K3 fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style K4 fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** In a typical KTU biomedical lab demonstration, students compare **bell vs. diaphragm chestpieces** of a stethoscope. The **bell** emphasizes **S3 and S4** (low frequencies) by transmitting them via direct skin contact, while the **diaphragm** filters out low frequencies using its mechanical resonance and emphasizes **S1, S2, and high-frequency murmurs** — perfectly mirroring the bell/diaphragm duality of clinical auscultation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define Phonocardiography. Mention any four normal heart sounds with their frequency ranges.**

**Model Answer (Point-wise for KTU valuation):**

- **Phonocardiography (PCG):** It is the technique of graphically recording the sounds produced by the heart during the cardiac cycle using a sensitive microphone, amplifier, and display system. *(1 mark)*
- The graphical record obtained is called a **Phonocardiogram**. *(0.5 marks)*
- **Four normal heart sounds and their frequency ranges:** *(1.5 marks)*

| Sound | Frequency Range |
|---|---|
| First heart sound (S1) | 25 – 45 Hz |
| Second heart sound (S2) | 50 – 80 Hz |
| Third heart sound (S3) | 25 – 35 Hz |
| Fourth heart sound (S4) | 15 – 30 Hz |

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: List any three types of microphones used in Phonocardiography. State the working principle of a piezoelectric microphone.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Three microphones used in PCG:** *(1.5 marks)*
  1. Piezoelectric microphone
  2. Electret condenser microphone
  3. Dynamic (moving-coil) microphone
- **Principle of piezoelectric microphone:** *(1.5 marks)*
  - It works on the **direct piezoelectric effect**.
  - When mechanical pressure (heart-sound vibration) is applied to a piezoelectric crystal such as **PZT (lead zirconate titanate)** or **PVDF polymer**, an electric charge $Q$ is generated across its faces.
  - This charge is proportional to the applied force: $Q = d \cdot F$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient.
  - The charge is converted to voltage by a **high-input-impedance FET preamplifier**, producing the PCG signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, broken into two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Students must answer **either the full Question A or the full Question B** (internal choice between the two). Each sub-part must be supported with diagrams where applicable.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]`

**Q: (a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a Phonocardiograph. Discuss in detail the role of each block in capturing, conditioning, and displaying heart sounds. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare the performance of piezoelectric, condenser, and dynamic microphones as PCG transducers. State, with justification, which one is preferred for modern clinical phonocardiographs. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

A phonocardiograph consists of the following cascaded blocks:

1. **Cardiac Microphone (Transducer):** Converts the acoustic vibrations of the heart (chest-wall-borne pressure waves) into an analog electrical signal. Common types: piezoelectric, electret, condenser. *(1 mark)*
2. **Preamplifier:** A low-noise, high-CMRR instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128) that boosts the μV-level transducer output to a workable mV level. Essential because heart sounds produce very small diaphragm displacements (nm scale). *(1 mark)*
3. **Band-Pass Filter Bank:** Splits the broadband PCG signal (20–2000 Hz) into three diagnostic channels:
   - Low-pass (~20–50 Hz) → isolates S3 and S4
   - Mid-band (~50–100 Hz) → isolates S1 and S2
   - High-pass (~100–2000 Hz) → isolates murmurs *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Main Amplifier with Gain Control:** Provides adjustable gain (typically 40–100 dB) so the signal fills the display range. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Display/Recorder:** Oscilloscope, thermal strip-chart recorder, or PC with DAQ card for real-time visualization and storage. *(1 mark)*
6. **Timing Reference:** Often synchronized with an ECG channel to temporally correlate mechanical and electrical cardiac events. *(1 mark)*
7. **Block diagram:** *(1 mark)* — Students must draw a labeled block diagram showing the signal flow from transducer → preamp → filters → main amp → display. *(See Section 4.1.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

| Parameter | Piezoelectric | Condenser / Electret | Dynamic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency Range | 0.1 Hz – 10 kHz ✅ | 20 Hz – 20 kHz ✅ | 50 Hz – 15 kHz ⚠ |
| Low-Frequency Sensitivity (S3, S4) | Excellent ✅ | Good ✅ | Poor ❌ |
| High-Frequency Response (Murmurs) | Excellent ✅ | Excellent ✅ | Moderate ⚠ |
| Output Impedance | Very High (FET preamp needed) | High (preamp needed) | Low (direct coupling) ✅ |
| External Power | Not required ✅ | Not required (electret) ✅ | Not required ✅ |
| Size & Portability | Compact ✅ | Very compact ✅ | Bulky ❌ |
| Cost | Medium | Low ✅ | High |
| Robustness | High ✅ | Moderate | High ✅ |
| Modern Clinical Preference | ✅ **Yes** | ✅ (Electronic stethoscopes) | ❌ (rarely) |

**Conclusion / Justification (preferred transducer):** *(2 marks)*
- The **piezoelectric microphone is the preferred PCG transducer** in modern clinical instruments because:
  1. It offers the **widest usable frequency response (0.1 Hz to >10 kHz)**, covering S3, S4 (very low freq) and murmurs (high freq) simultaneously.
  2. It exhibits the **highest sensitivity to small mechanical displacements**, critical for low-amplitude heart sounds.
  3. It is **rugged, compact, and requires no external power**, making it ideal for bedside and ambulatory use.
  4. Electret microphones are gaining ground in **electronic stethoscopes** due to cost and size advantages, but piezoelectric still dominates **diagnostic-grade clinical PCG systems**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]`

**Q: (a)** Describe the physiological origin, frequency range, duration, and clinical significance of S1, S2, S3, and S4 heart sounds. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a piezoelectric microphone. Derive the expression for the open-circuit output voltage. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Sound | Physiological Origin | Frequency Range | Duration | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** *(1.5 marks)* | Closure of mitral (M1) and tricuspid (T1) valves at the onset of ventricular systole | 25 – 45 Hz | ~0.14 s | Marks beginning of systole; loud in mitral stenosis, soft in mitral regurgitation |
| **S2** *(1.5 marks)* | Closure of aortic (A2) and pulmonary (P2) valves at the end of ventricular systole | 50 – 80 Hz | ~0.11 s | Marks end of systole; loud in systemic hypertension (A2), loud in pulmonary hypertension (P2); wide splitting seen in RBBB |
| **S3** *(1.5 marks)* | Rapid passive filling of ventricles in early diastole (vibrations from blood decelerating against ventricular wall) | 25 – 35 Hz | ~0.04 s | Normal in children/young adults; **pathological in adults** — indicates volume overload (e.g., CHF, mitral regurgitation) |
| **S4** *(1.5 marks)* | Atrial contraction forcing blood into a **non-compliant (stiff) ventricle** | 15 – 30 Hz | ~0.04 s | **Always pathological** — indicates conditions like **left ventricular hypertrophy, hypertension, aortic stenosis, ischemic heart disease** |
| **Diagrammatic correlation with ECG:** | Insert the PCG-ECG timing diagram (see Section 4.2) | — | — | Timing correlation with P-QRS-T is **essential** *(1 mark)* |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Construction of Piezoelectric Microphone (Sketch Description):** *(3 marks)*

```
       Acoustic Pressure
              ↓
      ┌─────────────────┐
      │  Thin Metallic  │   ← Diaphragm (vibrates with heart sound)
      │    Diaphragm    │
      ├─────────────────┤
      │  Piezoelectric  │   ← Crystal disc (PZT / PVDF / Quartz)
      │     Crystal     │
      ├─────────────────┤
      │  Base Electrode │
      └────────┬────────┘
               │
        Output Terminals (to FET preamp)
```

- A **thin metallic diaphragm** is mechanically coupled to a **piezoelectric crystal disc** (PZT or PVDF).
- The crystal is sandwiched between **two metallic electrodes** that tap off the generated charge.
- The assembly is housed in a metal case with an **acoustic port** for chest-wall placement.
- Output is fed to a **FET-input preamplifier** because the piezoelectric source impedance is extremely high.

**Working Principle:** *(1.5 marks)*

- When heart-sound vibrations strike the diaphragm, they transmit mechanical force to the piezoelectric crystal.
- The crystal undergoes **mechanical deformation (compression/shear)**.
- Due to the **direct piezoelectric effect**, equal and opposite charges appear on the two faces of the crystal.
- The magnitude of the generated charge is $Q = d \cdot F = d \cdot \sigma \cdot A$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient, $F$ is the applied force, $\sigma$ is the stress, and $A$ is the electrode area.
- This charge is converted to a usable voltage by a charge preamplifier.

**Derivation of Open-Circuit Output Voltage:** *(2.5 marks)*

Start with the parallel-plate capacitor model of the crystal:

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

where $t$ is the crystal thickness and $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity.

The charge generated is:

$$Q = d \cdot \sigma \cdot A$$

The open-circuit voltage is $V_{oc} = Q / C_0$:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{d \cdot \sigma \cdot A}{C_0} = \frac{d \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}$$

$$\boxed{V_{oc} = \frac{d \cdot \sigma \cdot t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} = g \cdot \sigma \cdot t}$$

where $g = d / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)$ is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** (V·m/N).

**Conclusion:** The open-circuit voltage is directly proportional to the applied stress and crystal thickness. Thicker crystals and higher mechanical stress produce larger PCG signal amplitudes — but thicker crystals also lower the resonant frequency, narrowing bandwidth. This is the **fundamental design trade-off** in PCG piezoelectric transducer engineering.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU PCG Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up frequencies of S3 and S4:** S3 is *higher* in frequency (25–35 Hz) than S4 (15–30 Hz). Many students swap these and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing S1 and S2 durations:** S1 (~0.14 s) is *longer* than S2 (~0.11 s) because the mitral/tricuspid closure is a more gradual event. Examiners specifically test this in 3-mark questions.
> 3. **Writing only the formula, not the derivation:** For piezoelectric questions, simply stating $V_{oc} = g \sigma t$ without deriving it from $Q = C_0 V_{oc}$ costs full marks. Show the algebraic steps.
> 4. **Forgetting the units in SPL calculations:** A 7-mark question on SPL must end with the answer in **dB SPL**, not just a number. Always re-attach the unit.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in Part B sub-questions:** A neat labeled block diagram is worth at least 1–2 marks. KTU examiners explicitly reward diagrams and penalize text-only answers.
> 6. **Not correlating PCG with ECG timing:** A 14-mark question on heart sounds without an ECG-synchronized timing diagram is considered incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Phonocardiography (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Phonocardiography (PCG)** = Graphical recording of heart sounds; instrument = **Phonocardiograph**; transducer = **Cardiac Microphone**.
- ✅ **Four normal heart sounds:** S1 (25–45 Hz, mitral/tricuspid closure, ~0.14 s), S2 (50–80 Hz, aortic/pulmonary closure, ~0.11 s), S3 (25–35 Hz, rapid ventricular filling, early diastole), S4 (15–30 Hz, atrial contraction, late diastole).
- ✅ **Murmur frequency band:** 100–1000 Hz — turbulent blood flow through stenotic or incompetent valves.
- ✅ **Most common PCG transducer:** **Piezoelectric microphone** (uses direct piezoelectric effect, $Q = d \sigma A$) — preferred for wide bandwidth, high sensitivity, no external power.
- ✅ **Condenser microphone** uses $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / x$; output in **hundreds of μV** — needs low-noise preamp.
- ✅ **Electret microphone** = permanently polarized dielectric version of condenser; used in modern electronic stethoscopes.
- ✅ **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P / P_0)$; reference $P_0 = 20 \ \mu Pa$.
- ✅ **PCG signal chain:** Transducer → Preamplifier (low-noise) → Band-Pass Filter Bank (3 channels) → Main Amplifier → Display.
- ✅ **S1 follows QRS complex; S2 follows T wave** — fundamental ECG-PCG timing relationship.
- ✅ **Typical PCG intensity at chest wall:** 45–60 dB SPL — well above 30 dB ambient.
- ✅ **Open-circuit voltage of piezoelectric crystal:** $V_{oc} = g \cdot \sigma \cdot t$.
- ✅ **Resonant frequency of crystal:** $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2t} \sqrt{E / \rho}$ — must be >> 2 kHz to ensure flat response.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 favourite viva question:** *"Why is a piezoelectric microphone preferred over a dynamic microphone for PCG?"* — Answer with bandwidth, sensitivity, low-frequency response, and size arguments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of EEG (Electroencephalogram - Neuronal Communication, EEG Measurement, Recording and Analysis)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT611 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Measurement of EEG (Electroencephalogram - Neuronal Communication, EEG Measurement, Recording and Analysis) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the electrical activity of the brain over time using multiple electrodes placed strategically on the scalp surface. The recorded voltage fluctuations (in microvolts, $\mu V$) represent the summed **postsynaptic potentials** of synchronously firing cortical pyramidal neurons, captured and displayed as a function of time. The EEG signal is generated by the brain's electrochemical activity and provides a real-time window into the functional state of the cerebral cortex. The **standard amplitude range** of an EEG signal lies between **10 $\mu$V and 300 $\mu$V**, with a **frequency content** spanning **0.5 Hz to 100 Hz**, although clinically significant bands lie between 0.5 Hz and 30 Hz. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The official KTU 2024 syllabus mandates coverage of *Neuronal Communication, EEG Measurement, Recording and Analysis* as a unified topic. Examiners specifically test the linkage between cellular neurophysiology (action potentials, synaptic currents) and the macroscopic scalp-recorded EEG. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Brain's Symphony Imagine you are standing outside a packed football stadium where 100,000 fans are talking simultaneously. Individually, you cannot hear any single conversation, but you *can* feel a collective "roar" that rises and falls in rhythm with the game. This collective roar is what an EEG measures. - **The crowd** = 86 billion neurons in the brain. - **Individual conversations** = single-cell action potentials (1 mV at the cell, but unmeasurable at the scalp). - **The collective roar** = synchronised **postsynaptic potentials** from thousands of vertically-oriented **pyramidal neurons** in the cortex. - **The microphone outside the stadium** = the scalp EEG electrode. - **The audio recorder** = the EEG amplifier and storage system. For the "roar" to be detectable, the fans (neurons) must clap or chant in *synchrony*. Only synchronised, spatially-aligned neural activity produces signals strong enough (tens of microvolts) to traverse the skull and reach the electrodes. ### 1.3 Neuronal Communication — The Cellular Origin of EEG EEG signals do **not** arise from action potentials directly (despite the common misconception). They arise from **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** at the dendrites and somas of **cortical pyramidal neurons**. | Cellular Event | Amplitude (at cell) | Reach Scalp Electrode? | Contribution to EEG | |---|---|---|---| | Single Action Potential | ~100 mV | No (too brief, ~1 ms) | Negligible | | Single Postsynaptic Potential (PSP) | ~10 mV | Marginal | Very small | | Synchronised PSPs from thousands of aligned pyramidal cells | ~10 mV locally | **Yes** | **Dominant source** | | Glial cell activity | Microvolts | No | None | > [!NOTE] > **Why Pyramidal Neurons?** The pyramidal cells of cortical layers III, V, and VI are uniquely aligned in a perpendicular orientation relative to the cortical surface. When thousands fire in unison, their dipoles sum vectorially, producing a measurable macroscopic field. Random orientations (as in deeper structures) result in cancellation. ### 1.4 Ionic Basis — The Nernst and Goldman Equations The resting membrane potential of a neuron is governed by ionic gradients. The **Nernst Equation** for a single ionic species X is given by: $$ E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) $$ For the more realistic multi-ion case, the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** accounts for the relative permeabilities of $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{K}^+$, and $\text{Cl}^-$: $$ V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_{K}[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_{K}[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right) $$ At body temperature ($37^\circ C$), $\frac{RT}{F} \approx 26.7$ mV, often rounded to **61.5 mV** when using $\log_{10}$ and the constant is absorbed ($\frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{F}$). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synaptic transmission** converts a presynaptic electrical signal into a chemical (neurotransmitter) signal, then back into a postsynaptic potential. This electrochemical-to-electrical conversion is what produces the slow (10–200 ms) PSPs that EEG electrodes can detect. The slowness is what allows temporal summation across many neurons. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Stages of EEG Measurement The end-to-end EEG signal chain involves the following structured logic steps: 1. **Signal Generation (Biophysical Origin):** Synchronised PSPs in cortical pyramidal neurons create a local field potential (LFP). 2. **Volume Conduction:** The signal passes through the cerebrospinal fluid (CSF), skull, and scalp — each acting as a volume conductor with different conductivity. 3. **Electrode-Skin Interface:** Ag/AgCl electrodes transduce ionic currents in the skin into electronic currents measurable by the amplifier. 4. **Amplification & Filtering:** Differential amplifiers (gain $\sim 10^4$) with high CMRR (Common Mode Rejection Ratio, ideally $> 100$ dB) amplify the microvolt-level signals while rejecting 50/60 Hz mains interference. 5. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC):** Sampling at 256–512 Hz; per the **Nyquist criterion**, sampling rate must be $\geq 2 \times f_{max}$. 6. **Signal Processing:** Digital filtering (bandpass 0.5–70 Hz), artefact rejection, and feature extraction. 7. **Display & Storage:** Waveform display (time domain), spectral plots (frequency domain), and topographic maps. 8. **Clinical Interpretation:** Visual inspection by a neurologist or automated analysis (e.g., seizure detection). ### 2.2 The Volume Conductor Problem The skull has a conductivity roughly **40–80 times lower** than that of the scalp and brain tissue. This attenuation and spatial smearing of cortical signals is a fundamental limitation of scalp EEG. The **electric field** generated by a current dipole **P** (modelling synchronised pyramidal neurons) located at depth $d$ below the scalp, with the scalp as a spherical volume conductor, can be approximated by: $$ V(r, \theta) = \frac{P \cos\theta}{4 \pi \sigma r^2} $$ where $V$ is the potential at scalp position $(r, \theta)$, $P$ is the dipole moment, and $\sigma$ is the effective conductivity of the medium. Signals decay with **the square of distance** — explaining why deep sources are difficult to localise. ### 2.3 International 10–20 Electrode Placement System The **10–20 system** is the universal standard for EEG electrode positioning. Electrode positions are determined as **10% or 20%** of specific skull landmarks: - **Nasion (Nz):** Front-most point of the frontal bone. - **Inion (Iz):** Bump at the back of the skull. - **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** In front of each ear. The total head circumference Nasion–Inion is divided into segments of 10% (edges) and 20% (intermediate positions). Standard electrode labels include **Fp1, Fp2, F3, F4, C3, C4, P3, P4, O1, O2, F7, F8, T3, T4, T5, T6, Fz, Cz, Pz** (odd = left, even = right, z = midline). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The reference problem:** EEG is inherently a *bipolar* measurement — it always records the potential **difference** between two electrodes. Common referencing schemes include *linked-ears reference (A1+A2)*, *average reference*, and *Cz reference*. The choice dramatically affects the appearance of the trace. ### 2.4 EEG Frequency Bands — The "Brainwave" Spectrum > [!NOTE] > KTU examiners frequently test knowledge of EEG frequency bands, their associated mental states, and clinical correlations. This is a high-yield topic. | Band | Frequency Range | Amplitude (typical) | Associated State | Clinical Significance | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Delta ($\delta$)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | 20 – 200 $\mu$V | Deep (slow-wave) sleep; infancy | Pathological in awake adults (tumours, encephalopathy) | | **Theta ($\theta$)** | 4 – 8 Hz | $< 30 \mu$V | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation | Temporal lobe epilepsy, memory encoding | | **Alpha ($\alpha$)** | 8 – 13 Hz | 20 – 60 $\mu$V | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed, occipital dominance | Attenuates on eye opening (Berger effect) | | **Beta ($\beta$)** | 13 – 30 Hz | $< 20 \mu$V | Active thinking, focused mental activity | Frontal dominance; anxiety, caffeine | | **Gamma ($\gamma$)** | $> 30$ Hz (up to 100 Hz) | Very low | Higher cognitive processing, perception binding | Reduced in Alzheimer's and schizophrenia | | **Mu ($\mu$)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Often similar to alpha | Sensorimotor cortex idling | Suppressed by movement or motor imagery (BCI) | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the high-yield reference set. Memorise these equations — they are recurrent in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations, both in Part A (3-mark) definitions and Part B (14-mark) numericals. | # | Concept | Equation | Description | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Nernst Potential | $E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$ | Equilibrium potential for a single ion | | 2 | GHK Voltage | $V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}\right)$ | Resting membrane potential | | 3 | Volume Conduction | $V(r, \theta) = \frac{P \cos\theta}{4\pi\sigma r^2}$ | Scalp potential from a current dipole | | 4 | Power Spectral Density | $S(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \vert \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} \vert^2$ | Energy distribution vs frequency (Welch's method used) | | 5 | Common Mode Rejection Ratio | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ dB | Quantifies noise rejection capability | | 6 | Nyquist Criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate for lossless digitisation | | 7 | Differential Gain | $V_{out} = A_d (V_+ - V_-) + A_{cm} \cdot \frac{V_+ + V_-}{2}$ | Output of a differential amplifier | | 8 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ dB | EEG systems aim for SNR $> 20$ dB | | 9 | Electrode-Skin Impedance | $Z = \vert Z \vert \angle \theta$ | Should be $< 5$ k$\Omega$ at 10 Hz for clinical EEG | | 10 | Fourier Transform (Discrete) | $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$ | Used to convert time-domain EEG to frequency domain | > [!TIP] > **Engineering utility:** EEG technology underpins Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCI), epilepsy monitoring, sleep staging (polysomnography), anaesthesia depth monitoring (BIS), neuromarketing, and cognitive workload assessment in human-factors engineering. The same theoretical framework extends to **ECoG** (electrocorticography, intracranial) and **MEG** (magnetoencephalography). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Nernst Equation from Thermodynamics The Nernst equation can be derived from the equality of the **electrochemical potential** on both sides of a permeable membrane. Consider an ionic species X with charge $z$ crossing the membrane. **Step 1: Chemical Potential Difference.** The chemical potential on the outside versus inside differs because of concentration: $$ \Delta \mu_{chem} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) $$ **Step 2: Electrical Potential Difference.** The work done moving charge $zF$ across a potential difference $V$ is: $$ \Delta \mu_{elec} = zFV $$ **Step 3: Equilibrium Condition.** At equilibrium (no net flux), the total electrochemical potential difference is zero: $$ \Delta \mu_{chem} + \Delta \mu_{elec} = 0 $$ $$ RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) + zFV = 0 $$ **Step 4: Solve for V (the Nernst Potential).** $$ V = E_X = -\frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}\right) $$ **Step 5: Convert to $\log_{10}$ form using $2.303 \log_{10}(x) = \ln(x)$.** $$ E_X = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}\right) $$ At $T = 310$ K (body temperature), substituting $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96485$ C/mol: $$ \frac{2.303 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 310}{96485} \approx 0.0615 \text{ V} = 61.5 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 6: Practical Form.** $$ E_X = \frac{61.5 \text{ mV}}{z} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{in}}{[X]_{out}}\right) $$ > [!NOTE] > For K⁺ ($z = +1$): with $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM, $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM, $E_K = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}(28) \approx -90.8$ mV. This is close to the resting membrane potential but not identical, because other ions contribute — necessitating the GHK equation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Volume Conduction — Scalp Potential from a Current Dipole A synchronised population of pyramidal neurons acts as an **equivalent current dipole** of moment $P = q \cdot d$ in a homogeneous volume conductor. **Step 1: Equipotential surface of a current dipole.** The potential at distance $r$ from a current dipole oriented at angle $\theta$ to the measurement axis in a homogeneous medium of conductivity $\sigma$ is given by Coulomb's law analogue for bioelectricity: $$ V(r, \theta) = \frac{1}{4\pi\sigma} \cdot \frac{P \cos\theta}{r^2} $$ **Step 2: Insertion of typical values.** Let $P = q \cdot d$. If $q = 1$ nA·m (representing synchronised dipoles) and $d = 100$ $\mu$m (cortex depth): $$ P = 10^{-9} \text{ A·m} \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 10^{-13} \text{ A·m}^2 $$ **Step 3: Substituting $\sigma \approx 0.3$ S/m (brain/scalp) and $r \approx 0.03$ m (3 cm from cortex to scalp):** $$ V = \frac{1}{4 \pi (0.3)} \cdot \frac{(10^{-13}) \cos\theta}{(0.03)^2} $$ $$ V = \frac{1}{3.77} \cdot \frac{10^{-13}}{9 \times 10^{-4}} \cos\theta $$ $$ V \approx 0.265 \cdot 1.11 \times 10^{-10} \cos\theta $$ $$ V \approx 2.95 \times 10^{-11} \cos\theta \text{ V} \approx 30 \text{ nV} \cdot \cos\theta $$ **Step 4: Why this is so small.** A single current dipole contributes only tens of nanovolts. To reach the **observable 10–100 $\mu$V** at the scalp, **roughly 10$^6$ to 10$^7$ synchronously firing dipoles** must align — which is why EEG primarily reflects mass synchronised activity rather than individual neuronal firing. > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation explains *why* EEG has low spatial resolution (limited by the physics of volume conduction) but excellent temporal resolution (millisecond-range). It also justifies the use of **larger electrodes (5–10 mm)** in clinical EEG — to improve SNR by averaging over a larger population of dipoles. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: CMRR Impact on 50 Hz Mains Interference The differential EEG amplifier has differential gain $A_d$ and common-mode gain $A_{cm}$. The output for an input of differential signal $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ and common-mode voltage $V_{cm} = \frac{V_+ + V_-}{2}$ is: $$ V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_d + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm} $$ **Step 1: Define CMRR.** $$ \text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert $$ **Step 2: Compute effective interference suppression.** If the differential EEG signal is $V_d = 50 \mu V$ and a 50 Hz mains interference appears as $V_{cm} = 1$ V common to both electrode leads, with $A_d = 10^4$ and $\text{CMRR} = 100$ dB (i.e., $A_d / A_{cm} = 10^5$): $$ A_{cm} = \frac{A_d}{\text{CMRR (linear)}} = \frac{10^4}{10^5} = 0.1 $$ **Step 3: Compute output contributions.** $$ V_{out,diff} = 10^4 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} = 0.5 \text{ V} $$ $$ V_{out,cm} = 0.1 \times 1 = 0.1 \text{ V} $$ **Step 4: Compute total output.** $$ V_{out} = 0.5 + 0.1 = 0.6 \text{ V} $$ **Step 5: Compute residual interference.** $$ \text{Residual interference} = \frac{V_{out,cm}}{V_{out,total}} = \frac{0.1}{0.6} = 16.7\% $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** With a 100 dB CMRR, mains interference is reduced to ~16.7% of the signal level. To bring it below 1%, CMRR must exceed ~120 dB — achievable only with high-quality instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., AD8221, INA128). For KTU 2024 Scheme numerical problems, the typical CMRR is in the range of **80–110 dB** for clinical EEG systems. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sources of EEG Artefacts and Mitigation > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners expect students to identify, characterise, and propose mitigation strategies for common EEG artefacts. Use the table below as a rapid reference. | Artefact Source | Frequency / Morphology | Affected Channels | Mitigation Technique | |---|---|---|---| | **Eye blinks (EOG)** | Slow, high-amplitude (200–500 $\mu$V), 0.5–3 Hz | Fp1, Fp2, frontal | Regression-based EOG subtraction; reject epochs | | **Eye movements (saccades)** | Step-like, low-frequency | Frontal leads | Independent Component Analysis (ICA) | | **Muscle (EMG)** | High-frequency (> 20 Hz), irregular | Temporal, frontal | Low-pass filtering; ask subject to relax | | **ECG/Cardiac ballistocardiogram** | Periodic ~1 Hz, sharp QRS-like | All (more in reference) | ICA component removal | | **Mains interference (50/60 Hz)** | Pure sinusoid | All | Notch filter; high CMRR; twisted leads | | **Sweat (electrodermal)** | Slow drift, $< 0.5$ Hz | Variable | High-pass filter (0.1–0.5 Hz cut-off) | | **Electrode pop / impedance change** | Abrupt step or spike | Single channel | Re-prepare electrode; check impedance $< 5$ k$\Omega$ | | **Movement** | Large, irregular | All | Accelerometer-based correction; reject epochs | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EEG Acquisition System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Brain: Cortical Pyramidal Neurons] --> B[Volume Conductor: CSF, Skull, Scalp] B --> C[Electrode-Skin Interface: Ag/AgCl Electrodes] C --> D[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 10^4, CMRR 100 dB] D --> E[Bandpass Filter
0.5 Hz to 70 Hz] E --> F[Notch Filter 50/60 Hz] F --> G[Analog to Digital Converter
Sampling 256-512 Hz] G --> H[Digital Signal Processing Unit
FFT, ICA, Filtering] H --> I[Display and Storage
Time Domain, Spectral, Topographic Maps] H --> J[Feature Extraction
Band Power, Coherence] J --> K[Clinical Interpretation
Neurologist / AI Classifier] style A fill:#FFD580 style B fill:#FFE5B4 style C fill:#FFF0D0 style D fill:#E0F0FF style E fill:#E0F0FF style F fill:#E0F0FF style G fill:#D0E0FF style H fill:#C0D0FF style I fill:#B0C0FF style J fill:#A0B0FF style K fill:#90A0FF ``` --- ### 4.2 International 10-20 Electrode Placement System (Top View) ```mermaid flowchart LR Nz[Nasion Nz] --> Fp1[Fp1
10 percent] Fp1 --> F3[F3
20 percent] F3 --> C3[C3
20 percent] C3 --> P3[P3
20 percent] P3 --> O1[O1
20 percent] O1 --> Iz[Inion Iz] Iz --> O2[O2
20 percent] O2 --> P4[P4
20 percent] P4 --> C4[C4

20 percent] F4 --> Fp2[Fp2
20 percent] Fp2 --> Nz Fp1 --- F7[F7] F7 --- T3[T3] T3 --- T5[T5] T5 --- O1 Fp2 --- F8[F8] F8 --- T4[T4] T4 --- T6[T6] T6 --- O2 F3 --- Fz[Fz] Fz --- F4 C3 --- Cz[Cz] Cz --- C4 P3 --- Pz[Pz] Pz --- P4 ``` --- ### 4.3 Signal Flow in a Single EEG Channel ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Electrode 1
Active] --> C[Differential Amplifier] B[Electrode 2
Reference] --> C C --> D[First Stage Gain
x10 to x100] D --> E[Second Stage Gain

and Buffer] H --> I[ADC

DSP and Storage] style A fill:#FFE5B4 style B fill:#FFE5B4 style C fill:#E0F0FF style D fill:#D0E5FF style E fill:#D0E5FF style F fill:#C0D5FF style G fill:#B0C5FF style H fill:#A0B5FF style I fill:#90A5FF style J fill:#8095FF ``` --- ### 4.4 EEG Signal Processing and Analysis Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw EEG Data] --> B[Preprocessing] B --> B1[Bandpass Filtering] B --> B2[Notch Filtering] B --> B3[Artefact Rejection] B1 --> C[Feature Extraction] B2 --> C B3 --> C C --> C1[Time Domain
Amplitude, RMS, Zero Crossing] C --> C2[Frequency Domain
FFT, PSD, Band Power] C --> C3[Spatial Domain
Coherence, Topography] C1 --> D[Classification and Interpretation] C2 --> D C3 --> D D --> D1[Clinical Decision
Seizure, Sleep Stage] D --> D2[BCI Application

Cognition, Emotion] ``` > [!TIP] > **Reading these diagrams in exams:** KTU 2024 examiners often ask students to "draw the block diagram of an EEG measurement system." The four-block minimum answer should include: **(1) Electrode**, **(2) Differential Amplifier**, **(3) Filter**, **(4) Display/Recorder.** Adding the ADC and Digital Processing blocks scores full marks. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: Define EEG and list the standard frequency bands. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Model Answer:** > **EEG (Electroencephalogram)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological recording of the brain's electrical activity, captured via electrodes placed on the scalp. The signals originate from synchronised postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons, with amplitudes in the range of **10–300 $\mu$V**. **Standard EEG Frequency Bands:** | Band | Range | Associated State | |---|---|---| | Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Deep sleep | | Theta ($\theta$) | 4 – 8 Hz | Drowsiness | | Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8 – 13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness | | Beta ($\beta$) | 13 – 30 Hz | Active thinking | | Gamma ($\gamma$) | $> 30$ Hz | Higher cognition | > [!TIP] > Examiners often test this with a one-line follow-up: "Which band is prominent during sleep?" — answer: **Delta**. --- #### Question 2: What is the role of Ag/AgCl electrodes in EEG recording? **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** Ag/AgCl (Silver/Silver-Chloride) electrodes serve as the **transducer** that converts ionic currents flowing in the body (across the electrode-skin interface) into measurable electronic currents at the amplifier input. **Key Reasons for Ag/AgCl preference:** - Forms a stable half-cell potential (low offset voltage). - Low and stable **electrode-skin impedance** (typically $< 5$ k$\Omega$ at 10 Hz). - Minimises **polarisation** effects (non-polarisable electrode). - Provides a low-noise, low-drift interface suitable for DC and low-frequency signals. The electrode gel (containing Cl⁻ ions) maintains ionic conductivity between skin and electrode, completing the circuit. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > Common mistakes in EEG-related 14-mark answers: > 1. **Confusing action potentials with postsynaptic potentials** as the source of EEG — *action potentials do not contribute directly*. > 2. **Omitting the 10–20 system** when asked about electrode placement. > 3. **Skipping units** in numericals (always specify Hz, $\mu$V, dB, k$\Omega$). > 4. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** showing signal flow — full marks require labelled arrows and functional annotations. > 5. **Forgetting the role of CMRR** in 50/60 Hz interference rejection. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks): Neuronal Communication and EEG Signal Origin** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with suitable diagrams how an action potential is generated in a neuron and discuss why postsynaptic potentials (and not action potentials) are the primary source of EEG signals. **Model Solution:** **(i) Action Potential Generation (4 marks):** A neuronal action potential (AP) is a brief, all-or-nothing depolarisation of the membrane, propagating along the axon. The mechanism follows the **Hodgkin-Huxley framework**: 1. **Resting state:** Membrane potential $\approx -70$ mV, maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase pump (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in). High $K^+$ permeability dominates. 2. **Depolarisation:** When the membrane is stimulated to threshold (about -55 mV), voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels open**, causing a rapid influx of Na⁺. The membrane potential reverses to about +30 mV. 3. **Repolarisation:** Na⁺ channels inactivate; voltage-gated **K⁺ channels open**, K⁺ efflux restores the negative potential. 4. **Hyperpolarisation & Refractory period:** K⁺ channels close slowly, briefly overshooting to about -80 mV. Absolute and relative refractory periods prevent backward propagation. 5. **Return to resting state** via the Na⁺/K⁺ pump. **Key ion flow magnitudes:** - Intracellular: $[K^+] = 140$ mM, $[Na^+] = 12$ mM, $[Cl^-] = 4$ mM. - Extracellular: $[K^+] = 5$ mM, $[Na^+] = 145$ mM, $[Cl^-] = 120$ mM. **(ii) Why PSPs and not APs generate EEG (3 marks):** | Feature | Action Potential | Postsynaptic Potential | |---|---|---| | Duration | 1–2 ms | 10–200 ms | | Magnitude (at cell) | ~100 mV | ~0.1–10 mV | | Source | Voltage-gated channels at axon hillock | Ligand-gated channels at dendrites/soma | | Temporal summation | None (all-or-none) | Strong (graded, summating) | | Spatial summation | None | Strong (across thousands of synapses) | | Reach scalp? | No — too brief, too localised | **Yes** — slow, summated, large population | | Alignment needed | None (single axon) | Pyramidal cells vertically aligned in cortex | The **summation of thousands of slow PSPs from synchronised, geometrically aligned pyramidal neurons** produces a dipolar field strong enough (tens of microvolts) to penetrate the skull and reach scalp electrodes. APs, being brief and non-synchronised across the population, are spatially and temporally averaged to near-zero by volume conduction. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles and compute the equilibrium potential for $K^+$ at body temperature (37°C) given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 5$ mM. **Model Solution:** **Derivation of Nernst Equation (4 marks):** Consider an ionic species X with charge $z$ permeable across a membrane. At equilibrium, the **electrochemical potential** is equal on both sides. **Step 1: Chemical potential difference (concentration gradient):** $$ \Delta \mu_{chem} = \mu_{out} - \mu_{in} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) $$ **Step 2: Electrical potential difference (charge movement):** $$ \Delta \mu_{elec} = zF(V_{in} - V_{out}) = -zF V_m $$ where $V_m = V_{in} - V_{out}$ is the membrane potential. **Step 3: Total electrochemical potential difference:** $$ \Delta \mu_{total} = RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) - zF V_m $$ **Step 4: At equilibrium, $\Delta \mu_{total} = 0$:** $$ RT \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) = zF V_m $$ **Step 5: Solve for $V_m = E_X$:** $$ E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) $$ **Step 6: Convert to $\log_{10}$ using $2.303 \log_{10}(x) = \ln(x)$:** $$ E_X = \frac{2.303 \cdot RT}{zF} \log_{10}\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right) $$ **Numerical Computation (3 marks):** For K⁺ at 37°C ($T = 310$ K), $z = +1$: $$ E_K = \frac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485} \log_{10}\left(\frac{5}{140}\right) $$ $$ E_K = \frac{5933.6}{96485} \log_{10}(0.0357) $$ $$ E_K = 0.0615 \times (-1.4472) $$ $$ E_K \approx -0.0890 \text{ V} = -89.0 \text{ mV} $$ **Result:** $E_K \approx -89$ mV, which is close to the resting membrane potential (the deviation arises because other ions also contribute — handled by the GHK equation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Marking tip:** A full 7-mark answer must include all 6 derivation steps, the unit conversions, and the explicit substitution. Skipping the equilibrium condition (Step 4) costs 1 mark. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks): EEG Recording System and Signal Analysis** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the block diagram of a complete EEG measurement system. Discuss the function of each block and the typical specifications (gain, CMRR, bandwidth). **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram (3 marks) — see Section 4.1.** The complete EEG system consists of the following blocks in sequence: **1. Electrode Block:** - Ag/AgCl disc electrodes placed on the scalp as per the **10–20 system**. - Function: Convert ionic currents to electronic signals. - Specification: Electrode-skin impedance $< 5$ k$\Omega$ at 10 Hz; offset voltage $< 100 \mu$V. **2. Input Protection and Buffer:** - Current-limiting resistors and input protection diodes. - Function: Protect the amplifier from electrostatic discharge (ESD) and defibrillation surges. **3. Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (IA):** - Three op-amp topology (e.g., AD8221, INA128). - Function: Reject common-mode noise (50 Hz mains, body potential) and amplify the small differential EEG signal. - Specifications: - **Gain:** $10^4$ (selectable) - **CMRR:** $\geq 100$ dB at 50 Hz - **Input Impedance:** $> 100$ M$\Omega$ - **Noise:** $< 0.5 \mu$V RMS (0.5–50 Hz) **4. Bandpass Filter:** - High-pass at 0.5 Hz (removes DC drift, sweat artefacts). - Low-pass at 70 Hz (removes EMG and high-frequency noise). - Function: Limit bandwidth to clinically relevant range. **5. Notch Filter:** - 50 Hz (India/Europe) or 60 Hz (USA) twin-T or digital notch. - Function: Reject mains interference. **6. Additional Gain Stage:** - Variable gain, typically 10–100×, to bring the signal to ADC input range (e.g., $\pm 5$ V). **7. Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** - Resolution: 16–24 bit. - Sampling rate: 256–1024 Hz (clinical EEG uses 256–512 Hz; research up to several kHz). - Per Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$, so 256 Hz safely captures 0.5–100 Hz content. **8. Digital Signal Processing (DSP):** - Functions: Digital filtering, artefact rejection, spectral analysis (FFT), feature extraction. - Modern systems use ICA, wavelet denoising, and machine learning. **9. Display and Storage:** - Real-time waveform display (time domain). - Spectral and topographic displays. - Storage in EDF (European Data Format) or BDF. **Complete Signal Chain Summary:** $$ \text{EEG (10–100 $\mu$V)} \xrightarrow{\times 10^4} \text{(1 V)} \xrightarrow{\text{filter}} \text{Clean signal} \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} \text{Digital data} \xrightarrow{\text{DSP}} \text{Clinical output} $$ --- **(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to the 10–20 electrode placement system, explain the procedure for positioning scalp electrodes. Also describe five common EEG artefacts and their mitigation strategies. **Model Solution:** **10–20 System Procedure (3 marks):** The 10–20 system is the international standard defined by the **International Federation of Clinical Neurophysiology (IFCN)**. It uses the following anatomical landmarks: 1. **Nasion (Nz):** Depression at the bridge of the nose, between the eyes. 2. **Inion (Iz):** Prominent bump at the back of the skull (external occipital protuberance). 3. **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** Depressions just in front of each ear canal. **Step-by-step placement:** - Measure the **Nasion-to-Inion distance** along the midline (e.g., 36 cm). Mark points at 10% from each end and 20% intervals in between. This places **Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz** along the midline. - Measure the **left-to-right pre-auricular distance** through Cz. Apply the same 10–20% rule to position **C3, Cz, C4** and laterally **T3, C3, C1, C2, C4, T4**. - Similar circumferential measurements define the **frontal, temporal, parietal, and occipital** rows. **Electrode Naming Convention:** - **Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7):** Left hemisphere. - **Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8):** Right hemisphere. - **"z" suffix (Cz, Fz, Pz):** Midline. - **Lower numbers (e.g., Fp1, Fp2):** Closer to midline; higher numbers (e.g., F7, F8): Lateral. A standard 10–20 montage uses 19 active electrodes + ground + reference, giving **21 channels** in clinical EEG. **Five Common EEG Artefacts and Mitigation (4 marks):** 1. **Eye Blinks (EOG):** - *Morphology:* Slow, high-amplitude (200–500 $\mu$V), 0.5–3 Hz transients; most prominent in **Fp1, Fp2**. - *Mitigation:* Instruct subject to minimise blinking; use **regression-based EOG subtraction** with a separately recorded EOG channel; reject contaminated epochs. 2. **Muscle Artefact (EMG):** - *Morphology:* High-frequency, irregular, spiky activity $> 20$ Hz; prominent in temporal and frontal leads. - *Mitigation:* Ask the subject to relax facial/jaw muscles; apply a **low-pass filter at 30–40 Hz**; use **ICA** to isolate and remove EMG components. 3. **50 Hz Mains Interference:** - *Morphology:* Pure sinusoidal contamination at the power-line frequency. - *Mitigation:* Use a **notch filter (50 Hz)**; ensure **CMRR $> 100$ dB**; use **twisted-pair leads**; keep electrode impedances balanced and $< 5$ k$\Omega$. 4. **Sweat / Slow Drift:** - *Morphology:* Very low-frequency baseline wander $< 0.5$ Hz. - *Mitigation:* Apply a **high-pass filter at 0.5 Hz**; maintain a cool recording environment; use abrasive gel to lower skin-electrode impedance. 5. **Cardiac Artefact (Pulse / BCG):** - *Morphology:* Periodic sharp transients synchronised with the ECG QRS complex. - *Mitigation:* Use **ICA** to identify and remove the cardiac component; alternative referencing (e.g., average reference) can minimise the effect. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final marks tip:** Always draw the **10–20 head schematic** with labels and arrow annotations, and present artefacts in a **tabular form** to save time. KTU examiners reward visual organisation generously in 14-mark answers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorise Before Every KTU Exam** - **EEG Definition:** Non-invasive recording of summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons, amplitude **10–300 $\mu$V**, bandwidth **0.5–100 Hz**. - **Source:** Postsynaptic potentials (PSPs), **NOT action potentials** — PSPs are slow (10–200 ms) and summate spatially and temporally. - **Neurons involved:** Pyramidal cells in cortical layers III, V, VI; geometrically aligned perpendicular to the scalp for dipole summation. - **Volume conduction:** Signal attenuates as $1/r^2$ through skull, CSF, and scalp; skull conductivity is ~40–80× lower than brain. - **Nernst equation:** $E_X = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\left(\frac{[X]_{out}}{[X]_{in}}\right)$; at 37°C, $\frac{2.303 RT}{F} = 61.5$ mV. - **GHK equation:** Accounts for multi-ion permeabilities; gives realistic resting potential (~ -70 mV). - **Five frequency bands:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (> 30 Hz).
