## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define Hounsfield Unit (CT number). List its values for air, fat, water, muscle, and cortical bone.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The CT number, also called the **Hounsfield Unit (HU)**, is a normalised measure of the linear attenuation coefficient of a tissue relative to that of water, scaled by a factor of 1000:

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

| Material | HU |
|---|---|
| Air | **−1000** |
| Fat | **−100 to −50** |
| Water | **0** |
| Muscle | **+35 to +55** |
| Cortical bone | **+700 to +3000** |

> *Allocation:* 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for table of values, 1 mark for explanation of normalisation choice.

---

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between 3rd generation and 4th generation CT scanners.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 3rd Generation | 4th Generation |
|---|---|---|
| X-ray tube movement | Rotates with detector | Rotates inside a stationary detector ring |
| Detector movement | Rotates with tube | Stationary (ring of 600–4800 detectors) |
| Beam geometry | Wide fan beam | Wide fan beam |
| Ring artefacts | Susceptible (detector drift) | Less susceptible |
| Scan time | 1–5 s | 1–5 s |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (more detectors) |

> *Allocation:* 1 mark per significant distinguishing feature, 3 marks total.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *Strict KTU pattern:* **Internal choice** — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A  `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **Part (a)** — Basic Principle with Block Diagram  *(7 marks)*

> **Q.** With a neat block diagram, explain the basic principle of Computed Tomography. List the major differences between CT and conventional radiography.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Principle (2 marks):**
Computed Tomography reconstructs a cross-sectional image of the body from a large number of X-ray transmission measurements taken at different angles. A thin fan-shaped or pencil-shaped X-ray beam rotates around the patient, and the attenuated beam is measured by detectors on the opposite side. The measurements are processed by a computer to yield an image of linear attenuation coefficients $\mu(x,y)$ for every voxel in the slice.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram; draw the same as a labelled rectangular block schematic in the answer book.)*

The essential blocks are: **High-voltage generator → X-ray tube → Collimator → Patient → Detector → Data Acquisition System (DAS) → Logarithmic amplifier → Reconstruction computer → Image memory → Display console.** The gantry controller, patient table, and operator console form the control hierarchy.

**Step 3 — Comparison with Conventional Radiography (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Conventional X-Ray | CT |
|---|---|---|
| Image type | 2-D projection (superimposed) | Cross-sectional (slice) |
| Depth resolution | None | Excellent |
| Soft-tissue contrast | Poor | Excellent |
| Radiation dose | Lower | Higher (multi-slice) |
| Source of image | Direct film/detector | Computer reconstruction |

---

#### **Part (b)** — Derivation of CT Number  *(7 marks)*

> **Q.** Derive the expression for the CT number in terms of the linear attenuation coefficients. A tissue voxel has $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = 0.025\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ and $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.020\,\text{cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the CT number.

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** *(See Section 3.3 for the complete derivation. The examiner expects the same five logical steps.)*

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}\;}$$

**Calculation (3 marks):**

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.025 - 0.020}{0.020}$$

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{0.005}{0.020}$$

$$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot 0.25$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{HU} = +250\;\text{Hounsfield Units}}$$

> *Interpretation:* A CT number of +250 HU corresponds to **densely packed soft tissue or early-calcified bone matrix** — a value clinically seen in thyroid calcifications, calcified granulomas, or early tooth enamel.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`  *(Alternative to Question A)*

#### **Part (a)** — Generations of CT Scanners  *(7 marks)*

> **Q.** Explain the different generations of CT scanners with their working principle, scan time, advantages, and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

**1st Generation — Pencil Beam, Translate–Rotate (2 marks)**
- **Principle:** A single pencil beam from the X-ray tube and a single detector translate linearly across the patient, acquire a profile, then rotate by 1° and repeat. A full 180° rotation yields 180 profiles.
- **Scan time:** 4–5 minutes per slice.
- **Advantage:** Simple, very low scatter, accurate.
- **Limitation:** Disastrously slow → not clinically viable except for brain-only static imaging.

**2nd Generation — Narrow Fan Beam, Translate–Rotate (1 mark)**
- **Principle:** Same as 1st gen, but with a narrow fan beam and 5–30 detectors.
- **Scan time:** 20–60 seconds.
- **Advantage:** Faster, less translation.
- **Limitation:** Still uses translation; scatter increases.

**3rd Generation — Wide Fan Beam, Rotate–Rotate (2 marks)**
- **Principle:** A wide fan beam covers the entire patient width. Both the X-ray tube and the curved detector array rotate together around the patient; no translation is required.
- **Scan time:** 1–5 seconds.
- **Advantage:** Faster, suitable for body imaging; widely used basis of modern scanners.
- **Limitation:** Subject to **ring artefacts** from mis-calibrated individual detectors.

**4th Generation — Rotate–Stationary (1 mark)**
- **Principle:** Only the X-ray tube rotates inside a **stationary full ring** of 600–4800 detectors.
- **Scan time:** 1–5 seconds.
- **Advantage:** No ring artefacts; detector calibration is more stable.
- **Limitation:** Expensive; susceptible to scatter because every detector sees the full fan.

**5th Generation — Electron Beam CT (EBCT) (1 mark)**
- **Principle:** No mechanical X-ray tube rotation. An electron gun sweeps a stationary tungsten anode ring, producing an X-ray fan beam that scans the patient in 50–100 ms.
- **Scan time:** 50–100 ms per slice.
- **Advantage:** Ultrafast, freezes cardiac motion.
- **Limitation:** Very expensive; largely displaced by multi-slice CT.

---

#### **Part (b)** — Image Reconstruction Methods  *(7 marks)*

> **Q.** Explain the two main classes of image reconstruction techniques used in CT. With a flow diagram, describe the Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm.

**Model Answer:**

**Class 1 — Analytical / Transform Methods (2 marks):**
The most important member is **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)**. It rests on the **Central Slice Theorem**: the 1-D Fourier transform of a projection at angle $\theta$ is a 1-D slice of the 2-D Fourier transform of the object at the same angle. The algorithm:

1. Acquire projections at many angles.
2. Take the 1-D Fourier transform of each projection.
3. Multiply by a **ramp filter** in the frequency domain (to compensate for the $1/r$ blurring of simple back-projection).
4. Take the inverse 1-D Fourier transform → *filtered projection*.
5. **Back-project** (smear) the filtered projection across the image grid at the corresponding angle.
6. Sum all back-projections → reconstructed image.

> **Advantage:** Computationally fast; analytically exact in the noise-free continuous limit.
> **Limitation:** Amplifies high-frequency noise; produces streak artefacts at low-dose acquisitions.

**Class 2 — Iterative / Algebraic Methods (2 marks):**
The image is reconstructed by **iteratively refining an initial estimate** until the forward-projected synthetic data match the measured projection data within tolerance. Variants include:

- **ART (Algebraic Reconstruction Technique)** — solves linear equations one at a time.
- **SIRT (Simultaneous Iterative Reconstruction Technique)** — updates using all equations simultaneously.
- **Statistical methods** — MLEM, MAP, ASIR, MBIR, ADMIRE — explicitly model photon statistics (Poisson noise).

> **Advantage:** Superior noise handling, allows incorporation of prior information, enables dose reduction.
> **Limitation:** Computationally expensive; long reconstruction times (mitigated by GPU acceleration).

**Flow Diagram of FBP (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram; reproduce as a labelled block diagram in the answer book.)*

The diagram should show: **Projections → 1-D FT → Ramp Filter → IFT → Back-projection → Image**, with an alternative branch showing the **iterative path** as a feedback loop.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on CT Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 1000** in the CT number formula → lose 2 marks instantly. Always write the full formula: $1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$.
> 2. **Confusing Hounsfield units with linear attenuation coefficient** — they are *not the same*. HU is *normalised*; $\mu$ is *absolute* (cm⁻¹).
> 3. **Mixing up 3rd and 4th generation scanners** — in 3rd gen the *detector rotates*; in 4th gen the *detector is stationary* (only the tube rotates).
> 4. **Skipping the unit in numerical answers** — always state "**HU**" or "**cm⁻¹**" explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** — arrows are *mandatory* to indicate information flow. A box without an arrow = 0 marks for that block.
> 6. **Writing "Filtered Back Projection" without explaining the ramp filter** — examiners specifically look for the term "**Central Slice Theorem**" + "**ramp filter**" as a pair. Missing either one costs 1.5 marks.
> 7. **Forgetting to state that water = 0 HU by definition** — a 1-mark trap question that catches students who memorise the formula but not its design.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision? Read this list aloud — it covers 90 % of marks.**

### A. Core Concept Block
- [x] CT = **cross-sectional X-ray imaging** using computer reconstruction.
- [x] Solves the **superimposition problem** of plain radiography.
- [x] Hounsfield scale: **water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU, bone = +1000 HU** (approx).
- [x] Nobel Prize 1979 — **Hounsfield & Cormack**.

### B. Mathematical Toolbox
- [x] Beer–Lambert law: $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$.
- [x] Cascade for N tissues: $I = I_0 \exp\!\left(-\sum \mu_i x_i\right)$.
- [x] Log-domain projection: $P = \ln(I_0/I) = \int \mu(x,y)\,ds$.
- [x] **CT number formula:** $\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ — *memorise verbatim*.
- [x] Inverse problem: projections → image, solved by **FBP** (Central Slice Theorem) or **iterative methods**.

### C. System Architecture
- [x] Major blocks: **HV generator → X-ray tube → collimator → patient → detector → DAS → log amp → reconstruction CPU → image memory → display**.
- [x] Two control hierarchies: **gantry control** (rotation, tilt) and **table control** (axial or helical feed).
- [x] Slip-ring technology enables **continuous rotation** (helical/spiral CT).

### D. Generations — One-Line Memory Hooks
- [x] **1st** — pencil, translate–rotate, 1 detector, **minutes**.
- [x] **2nd** — narrow fan, translate–rotate, 5–30 detectors, **tens of seconds**.
- [x] **3rd** — wide fan, rotate–rotate, 256+ detectors, **seconds** ⭐ most clinically important.
- [x] **4th** — wide fan, tube rotates, **detector ring stationary**, seconds.
- [x] **5th** — **EBCT**, no moving parts, 50–100 ms, cardiac.
- [x] **6th** — **helical/spiral**, continuous table feed.
- [x] **7th** — **multi-detector row (MDCT)**, 64–320 rows, cone beam.

### E. Reconstruction Methods
- [x] **FBP** = filter (ramp) + back-project; fast, classic.
- [x] **Iterative (ART, SIRT, MLEM, MBIR)** = model-based; better noise, slower.
- [x] **Central Slice Theorem** = 1-D FT of projection = radial slice of 2-D object spectrum.

### F. Display & Post-Processing
- [x] **Window** = width W controls contrast; **Level** = centre L controls brightness.
- [x] Pixel grey level: $g = \dfrac{\mathrm{HU} - (L - W/2)}{W} \times 255$.
- [x] DICOM standard for storage; PACS for archival.

### G. Clinical & Engineering Pearls
- [x] Higher kVp → more Compton, less photoelectric → lower contrast but lower dose.
- [x] Iodine contrast agent (Z = 53) → strong photoelectric enhancement → vascular opacification.
- [x] Metal implants cause **streak/star artefacts** because HU saturates > +3000.
- [x] CT dose metrics: **CTDI** (mGy) and **DLP** (mGy·cm); cumulative effective dose in mSv.

> 🎯 **Final Examiner Tip:** Always pair every equation with its *physical meaning* in one sentence. KTU examiners reward *understanding*, not just formula recall. A student who writes "$\mathrm{HU} = 1000 \cdot (\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$ because we normalise tissue attenuation to water as the universal reference, scaled by 1000 to give bone an intuitive value of +1000" scores higher than one who writes only the equation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System (Radiation Therapy)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEBRT611 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Resonance Imaging System and Nuclear Medicine System (Radiation Therapy)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) System

### Formal Definition
**Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** is a non-invasive, non-ionizing diagnostic imaging modality that exploits the quantum mechanical property of **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR)** of hydrogen protons (¹H) — abundant in human tissue as water and fat — to generate high-resolution, multi-planar, tomographic images reflecting the spatial distribution of tissue relaxation properties (T1, T2) and proton density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** MRI uses a strong static magnetic field (typically **0.5 T to 3 T** clinically, up to **7 T** in research), orthogonal gradient fields, and radiofrequency (RF) pulses at the **Larmor frequency** to encode spatial information into the NMR signal.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine spinning tops (hydrogen protons) randomly oriented in your body. When you place them inside a giant magnet (the MRI bore), they all align along the magnetic field like compass needles. Now, a brief radio "shout" (RF pulse) knocks them off-axis. As they wobble back to alignment, each one emits a tiny radio "echo." By listening carefully to these echoes and knowing exactly where each echo came from (using gradient fields), a computer reconstructs a detailed image — almost like echolocation in bats, but using radio waves and magnetic fields instead of sound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants in MRI:**
> - Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen: **γ = 42.58 MHz/T**
> - At 1.5 T field, Larmor frequency ≈ **63.86 MHz**
> - Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**

---

## 1.2 Nuclear Medicine System

### Formal Definition
**Nuclear Medicine** is a medical specialty that uses **radiopharmaceuticals** (radioactively labeled compounds) administered to the patient for the diagnosis, staging, and treatment of disease. Imaging systems detect the gamma rays (for diagnosis) or particulate emissions (for therapy) emitted from the radiotracer to map physiological and metabolic function — often *before* anatomical changes become visible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Common imaging devices include the **Gamma Camera (Scintillation / Anger Camera)**, **SPECT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography)**, and **PET (Positron Emission Tomography)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a "Glow-in-the-dark" tag attached to a sugar molecule. You inject the glowing sugar into the patient, and it travels wherever real sugar goes (e.g., a hungry brain tumor). A special camera outside the body detects the glow and paints a picture of *where the sugar accumulates*. This reveals *function* (metabolism) rather than just structure — a tumor lights up long before it would show on an X-ray.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Radiopharmaceuticals:**
> - **Tc-99m** (140 keV gamma, t½ = 6 hours) — most widely used diagnostic isotope
> - **F-18 FDG** (511 keV annihilation photons, t½ = 110 min) — PET tracer
> - **I-131** (β⁻ and gamma emitter) — both diagnostic and therapeutic

---

## 1.3 Radiation Therapy (Radiotherapy)

### Formal Definition
**Radiation Therapy** is the medical use of **ionizing radiation** (high-energy photons, electrons, protons, or heavy ions) to treat malignant tumors (and occasionally benign conditions) by damaging the DNA of cancerous cells, inhibiting their proliferation, and inducing **apoptosis** (programmed cell death) while minimizing dose to surrounding healthy tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Major modalities include **External Beam Radiotherapy (EBRT)** using Linear Accelerators (LINAC), **Cobalt-60 Teletherapy**, **Brachytherapy** (internal sealed sources), and **Stereotactic Radiosurgery (SRS/SRT)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a magnifying glass focusing sunlight to burn a leaf (the tumor) while the surrounding grass (healthy tissue) is in the penumbra and receives only mild, recoverable heat. In radiotherapy, multiple weak beams from different angles converge on the tumor, each too weak to harm tissue it passes through, but their combined intensity at the target is lethal to cancer cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Radiotherapy Units & Constants:**
> - Absorbed dose: **Gray (Gy)** = 1 J/kg
> - Equivalent/Human effective dose: **Sievert (Sv)**
> - Activity: **Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration/second
> - Speed of light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**

---

## 1.4 Comparative Overview Table

| Parameter | MRI | Nuclear Medicine (SPECT/PET) | Radiation Therapy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | RF + Magnetic Field | Gamma rays / Annihilation photons | High-energy X-rays / γ / particles |
| **Ionizing?** | No | Yes (low dose) | Yes (therapeutic dose) |
| **Information Type** | Anatomical + Soft tissue contrast | Functional / Metabolic | Therapeutic |
| **Typical Field** | 0.5 – 3 T | N/A (uses detectors) | 6 – 25 MV photon beams |
| **Key Parameter** | Larmor frequency (MHz) | Tracer activity (MBq) | Absorbed dose (Gy) |
| **Image Basis** | Proton density, T1, T2 | Radiotracer biodistribution | Tumor target volume |
| **Spatial Resolution** | ~1 mm | 4 – 10 mm (SPECT) / 4 – 6 mm (PET) | Sub-mm (stereotactic) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 MRI System — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Physics of Nuclear Magnetic Resonance

The hydrogen nucleus (¹H, single proton) possesses **spin quantum number I = ½** and an associated magnetic moment **μ**. When placed in an external static magnetic field **B₀** (conventionally along the z-axis), the proton's magnetic moment precesses around B₀ at a characteristic angular frequency given by the **Larmor Equation**.

**Step 1 — Net Magnetization Alignment:** In thermal equilibrium, a slight excess of protons align *with* B₀ (lower energy state, spin-up) over those aligned *against* B₀ (higher energy, spin-down), governed by the Boltzmann distribution. This produces a net macroscopic magnetization vector **M₀** parallel to B₀.

**Step 2 — RF Excitation:** An RF pulse at the Larmor frequency, B₁(t), is applied perpendicular to B₀. This tips the net magnetization away from the z-axis by a **flip angle α**. Common flip angles:
- **90° pulse** — maximizes transverse magnetization (Mxy)
- **180° pulse** — inverts Mz (used in spin-echo sequences)

**Step 3 — Relaxation:** After RF excitation, the magnetization returns to equilibrium via two independent, simultaneous processes:
- **T1 Recovery (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice):** Mz regrows from 0 → M₀
- **T2 Decay (Transverse / Spin-Spin):** Mxy decays from maximum → 0

**Step 4 — Spatial Encoding:** Three orthogonal gradient coils (Gx, Gy, Gz) are used:
- **Slice-select gradient (Gss)** — selects a 2D slice during RF excitation
- **Frequency-encoding gradient (Gfe)** — along read-out direction (x)
- **Phase-encoding gradient (Gpe)** — along y, applied in steps

**Step 5 — Signal Reception & Reconstruction:** The precessing transverse magnetization induces a voltage in receiver RF coils (Faraday's Law). The signal — sampled in **k-space** — is inverse-Fourier-transformed to form the image.

### 2.1.2 T1 and T2 Relaxation Mechanism

- **T1 (longitudinal):** Energy transfer from excited protons to surrounding molecular lattice ("spin-lattice"). Short T1 → bright signal (e.g., fat). Long T1 → dark signal (e.g., CSF, water).
- **T2 (transverse):** Dephasing of proton spins due to local magnetic field inhomogeneities ("spin-spin"). Short T2 → rapid signal decay. Long T2 → bright signal (e.g., CSF).
- **T2\*** (T2-star): Includes macroscopic field inhomogeneities; basis of functional MRI (BOLD contrast).

### 2.1.3 Pulse Sequences
- **Spin Echo (SE):** 90° → 180° refocusing pulse → Echo at TE. T2-weighted.
- **Gradient Echo (GRE):** Variable flip angle < 90°, no 180° pulse. T2*-weighted, faster.
- **Inversion Recovery (IR):** 180° → 90° → 180°. Provides heavy T1 contrast (e.g., FLAIR, STIR).
- **Echo Planar Imaging (EPI):** Ultra-fast, used in diffusion and fMRI.

### 2.1.4 Key MRI Subsystems
1. **Superconducting Magnet** — generates B₀ (cooled by liquid He at 4 K)
2. **Gradient Coils (X, Y, Z)** — produce linear spatial gradients (~40 mT/m)
3. **RF Coils** — transmit/receive (body coil, head coil, surface phased arrays)
4. **Computer & Console** — pulse sequence control, image reconstruction
5. **Patient Handling System** — couch, bore, RF shielding (Faraday cage)

---

## 2.2 Nuclear Medicine System — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Radioactive Decay Principles

A radiopharmaceutical decays following the **exponential decay law**. The number of radioactive nuclei N(t) and activity A(t) decrease with time:

- **A(t) = A₀ · e^(-λt)** where λ is the decay constant
- **N(t) = N₀ · e^(-λt)**
- **Half-life:** t½ = ln(2) / λ ≈ **0.693 / λ**

### 2.2.2 Gamma Camera (Anger Camera) Components

1. **Collimator** — Lead plate with parallel/tapered holes; defines spatial origin of gamma rays (acts like a lens, but in reverse)
2. **Scintillation Crystal** — Typically NaI(Tl), 9.5 mm thick; converts gamma photons to visible light
3. **Photomultiplier Tubes (PMTs)** — Hexagonal array (typically 37, 61, or 91 PMTs) that convert scintillation light to electrical pulses
4. **Position Computer** — Computes (X, Y) centroid of light distribution (Anger positioning logic)
5. **Pulse Height Analyzer (PHA)** — Selects only the photopeak (e.g., 140 keV ± 10% for Tc-99m)
6. **Display & Computer** — Forms the planar image

### 2.2.3 SPECT — Single Photon Emission CT
- Gamma camera **rotates 360°** around the patient in 64–128 projections
- Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) or iterative reconstruction (MLEM/OSEM) yields 3D tomographic images
- Resolution: ~8–10 mm
- Common tracers: Tc-99m HMPAO (brain), Tc-99m MDP (bone), Tl-201 (myocardium)

### 2.2.4 PET — Positron Emission Tomography
- Uses β⁺ emitters (F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15, Ga-68)
- Positron annihilates with electron → **two 511 keV photons at 180°** (coincidence detection)
- **Time-of-Flight (TOF) PET** uses timing difference to localize annihilation along LOR
- Resolution: 4–6 mm
- SUV (Standardized Uptake Value) quantifies tracer uptake

---

## 2.3 Radiation Therapy — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.3.1 Interaction of Ionizing Radiation with Tissue

When high-energy photons enter tissue, the dominant interactions are:
- **Photoelectric Effect** (dominant at low E, ~Z³/E³)
- **Compton Scattering** (dominant at 0.1–10 MeV; major contributor in MV therapy)
- **Pair Production** (E > 1.022 MeV; in linac beams)

For particulate radiation:
- **Electrons** — Coulomb interactions, ionization, bremsstrahlung
- **Protons/Heavy ions** — Bragg peak (energy deposited at end of range)

### 2.3.2 Dose Distribution Concepts
- **PDD (Percentage Depth Dose):** Dose at depth d ÷ Dose at reference depth (usually dmax)
- **Isocenter:** Point of beam convergence; gantry, collimator, and couch rotate about it
- **TPS (Treatment Planning System):** Computes 3D dose distribution from CT data
- **ICRU 50/62 Volumes:** GTV, CTV, PTV, OAR — define target and organs at risk

### 2.3.3 Teletherapy vs Brachytherapy

| Modality | Source Location | Dose Rate | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cobalt-60 Teletherapy** | External (1.25 MeV γ) | ~1.5 Gy/min | Palliative, developing countries |
| **LINAC (6–25 MV)** | External X-ray beam | 2–10 Gy/min | Curative conformal therapy |
| **LDR Brachytherapy** | Implanted seeds (I-125, Pd-103) | 0.4–2 Gy/hr | Prostate |
| **HDR Brachytherapy** | Ir-192 afterloading | > 12 Gy/hr | Gyn, breast, lung |
| **Gamma Knife** | 201 Co-60 sources, focused | Single fraction | Brain SRS |
| **CyberKnife** | Robotic 6 MV linac | Hypofractionated | Body SRS/SBRT |

### 2.3.4 Linear Accelerator (LINAC) Subsystems
1. **Electron Gun** — Thermionic emission → pulsed electron beam
2. **Klystron / Magnetron** — RF source at **2.856 GHz (S-band)** or 9.3 GHz (X-band); accelerates electrons to MeV energies
3. **Waveguide / Accelerating Structure** — Travelling wave or standing wave
4. **Bending Magnet** — 270° achromatic bend for isocentric mounting
5. **Target** — High-Z (W, Pt) for bremsstrahlung X-ray production
6. **Flattening Filter** — Beam intensity modulation
7. **Multi-Leaf Collimator (MLC)** — Up to 120 leaves; shapes beam conformally
8. **Portal Imager / EPID** — Electronic verification
9. **Treatment Couch** — 6-DOF robotic

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Larmor Frequency** | $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ | γ in MHz/T, B₀ in Tesla, f₀ in MHz | MRI resonant frequency |
| 2 | **Gyromagnetic Ratio (¹H)** | $\gamma = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T}$ | Constant | Proton resonance |
| 3 | **Angular Larmor Frequency** | $\omega_0 = 2\pi\gamma B_0$ | rad/s | Bloch equation solution |
| 4 | **Larmor Wavelength** | $\lambda = c / f_0$ | c = 3×10⁸ m/s | RF coil sizing |
| 5 | **T1 Recovery (Bloch)** | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | Mz in A/m | Longitudinal regrowth |
| 6 | **T2 Decay (Bloch)** | $M_{xy}(t) = M_{xy}(0) \cdot e^{-t/T_2}$ | Mxy in A/m | Transverse decay |
| 7 | **MRI Signal Equation (SE)** | $S \propto \rho \cdot e^{-TE/T_2} \cdot (1 - e^{-TR/T_1})$ | ρ = proton density | Image contrast |
| 8 | **Radioactive Decay Law** | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | A in Bq, λ in s⁻¹ | Tracer activity |
| 9 | **Decay Constant** | $\lambda = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$ | t½ in seconds | Calculate λ |
| 10 | **Half-Life Relation** | $t_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$ | seconds | Calculate half-life |
| 11 | **Activity in Curies** | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Conversion | Activity unit |
| 12 | **Exposure Rate (Point Source)** | $\dot{X} = \Gamma A / d^2$ | Γ = specific gamma constant, d in m | Brachy/Tele |
| 13 | **Inverse Square Law** | $I \propto 1/d^2$ | Intensity vs distance | Beam profile |
| 14 | **Mass Energy Absorption** | $D = \Psi \cdot (\mu_{en}/\rho)$ | D = dose, Ψ = energy fluence | Dosimetry |
| 15 | **Dose Rate (Cobalt-60)** | $\dot{D} \approx 1.33 \text{ Gy·m²/Ci·h at 1 m}$ | Empirical constant | Teletherapy output |
| 16 | **PDD Definition** | $PDD(d) = D_d / D_{d_{max}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless % | Depth dose |
| 17 | **Inverse Square Correction** | $D_2 = D_1 (d_1/d_2)^2$ | Distance d | SSD changes |
| 18 | **Linear Energy Transfer (LET)** | $LET = dE/dx$ | keV/μm | Beam quality |
| 19 | **Oxygen Enhancement Ratio (OER)** | $OER = D_{hypoxic}/D_{oxic}$ | Ratio (2.5–3 for X-rays) | Radiobiology |
| 20 | **SUV (PET)** | $SUV = C_{tissue} / (A_{inj} / BW)$ | Concentration, activity, body weight | PET quantification |
| 21 | **Bragg Peak Energy** | $R \propto E^{1.7}$ for protons | Range R, Energy E | Proton therapy |
| 22 | **Annihilation Photon Energy** | $E = 2 m_e c^2 = 1.022 \text{ MeV}$ | Constant | PET physics |
| 23 | **Coincidence Time Window** | $\Delta t \approx 6 - 12 \text{ ns}$ | nanoseconds | PET detection |
| 24 | **RF Penetration Depth** | $\delta = \sqrt{2\rho / (\omega\mu_r\mu_0)}$ | Skin depth | RF coil design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** You will almost certainly be tested on the **Larmor equation** ($f_0 = \gamma B_0$) and the **radioactive decay equation** ($A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$). Memorize these and the relaxation equations (T1, T2) cold.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Larmor Frequency

The Larmor frequency is the cornerstone of all MRI physics. We derive it rigorously from classical mechanics and quantum physics perspectives.

### 3.1.1 Classical (Torque) Derivation

**Step 1 — Magnetic Moment of a Proton**
A hydrogen nucleus (single proton) has a magnetic moment vector **μ** proportional to its spin angular momentum **L**:
$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$$
where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio.

**Step 2 — Torque in a Magnetic Field**
The torque experienced by a magnetic moment in an external magnetic field $\vec{B_0}$ is given by:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$
This torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum (Newton's second law for rotation):
$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Equating and Expanding**
Equating the two expressions and substituting $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \vec{L}$:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})$$

**Step 4 — Larmor Precession Solution**
Assume the magnetic field $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$ and that the magnetization precesses around it. The solution to this differential equation reveals that $\vec{\mu}$ precesses about the z-axis with angular frequency:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

**Step 5 — Converting to Frequency in Hz**
The cyclic frequency in Hz (cycles per second) is:
$$f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For hydrogen-1 (¹H):** γ = 2.675 × 10⁸ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹, so γ/2π = **42.58 MHz/T**.

### 3.1.2 Worked Example: Larmor Frequency at 1.5 T

Given: $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T, $B_0 = 1.5$ T

$$f_0 = \gamma \cdot B_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}}$$

At **3 T** (clinical high-field): $f_0 = 42.58 \times 3 = 127.74$ MHz.

At **7 T** (research): $f_0 = 42.58 \times 7 = 298.06$ MHz.

### 3.1.3 Quantum Mechanical Justification
The Zeeman effect splits the two spin states of a proton (m = +½ and m = −½) by an energy difference:
$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0$$
where $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck constant. The energy of the RF photon needed to flip the spin must equal ΔE:
$$h f_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0 \implies f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

This **identical** result confirms the classical-quantum consistency of the Larmor equation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of MRI Signal Equation (Spin-Echo)

The **Spin-Echo signal intensity** is the foundation of contrast in T1- and T2-weighted imaging. We derive it step-by-step from the Bloch equations.

### 3.2.1 The Bloch Equations
The general Bloch equations in the rotating frame, including relaxation, are:
$$\frac{dM_x}{dt} = \gamma (M_y B_z - M_z B_y) - \frac{M_x}{T_2}$$
$$\frac{dM_y}{dt} = \gamma (M_z B_x - M_x B_z) - \frac{M_y}{T_2}$$
$$\frac{dM_z}{dt} = \gamma (M_x B_y - M_y B_x) - \frac{M_z - M_0}{T_1}$$

### 3.2.2 Spin-Echo Sequence Timing
A standard spin-echo sequence is parameterized by two intervals:
- **TR (Repetition Time):** Time between successive 90° excitation pulses
- **TE (Echo Time):** Time between the 90° pulse and the peak of the spin echo

### 3.2.3 Deriving the Signal Expression

**Step 1 — Effect of the 90° Pulse**
Immediately after a 90° pulse, the longitudinal magnetization $M_z$ becomes zero, and the transverse magnetization equals $M_0$.

**Step 2 — T1 Recovery During TR**
During the time TR (between successive 90° pulses), the longitudinal magnetization recovers according to:
$$M_z(TR) = M_0 (1 - e^{-TR/T_1})$$

This is the available longitudinal magnetization before the next 90° pulse.

**Step 3 — T2 Decay During TE**
After the second 90° pulse (or after the 180° refocusing pulse, in a spin-echo), the transverse magnetization decays during TE:
$$M_{xy}(TE) = M_0 (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) \cdot e^{-TE/T_2}$$

**Step 4 — Including Proton Density**
Multiplying by proton density $\rho$ (number of mobile hydrogen protons per unit volume):
$$\boxed{S = k \cdot \rho \cdot (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) \cdot e^{-TE/T_2}}$$

where k is a system constant including coil sensitivity, voxel volume, and instrument gain.

### 3.2.4 Contrast Analysis from the Signal Equation
- **T1-weighted image:** Short TR, Short TE — contrast dominated by $(1 - e^{-TR/T_1})$
- **T2-weighted image:** Long TR, Long TE — contrast dominated by $e^{-TE/T_2}$
- **Proton density weighted:** Long TR, Short TE — contrast dominated by ρ

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Radioactive Decay Equation

### 3.3.1 First-Order Decay
The rate of disintegration (activity) is proportional to the number of remaining radioactive nuclei:
$$-\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N$$

where λ is the decay constant (probability per unit time per atom).

### 3.3.2 Separating Variables and Integrating
$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

Integrating from $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N(t)$ at time t:
$$\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

$$\ln N(t) - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t$$

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### 3.3.3 Activity Form
Since activity $A = \lambda N$:
$$\boxed{A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### 3.3.4 Half-Life Relation
At $t = t_{1/2}$, $A = A_0/2$:
$$\frac{A_0}{2} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda t_{1/2}}$$

Taking natural log:
$$\ln(1/2) = -\lambda t_{1/2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}}$$

### 3.3.5 Mean Lifetime
The mean (average) lifetime of a radioactive nucleus:
$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{t_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \, t_{1/2}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Tc-99m Decay

A patient is administered **800 MBq of Tc-99m** for a bone scan. **How much activity remains 4 hours after elution?** Given: $t_{1/2}$(Tc-99m) = **6.02 hours**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the decay constant**
$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{6.02} = 0.1151 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the decay equation at t = 4 h**
$$A(4) = 800 \cdot e^{-0.1151 \times 4}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the exponent**
$$-0.1151 \times 4 = -0.4604$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential**
$$e^{-0.4604} = 0.6311$$

**Step 5 — Final result**
$$A(4) = 800 \times 0.6311 = 504.9 \text{ MBq}$$

$$\boxed{A(4 \text{ hr}) \approx 505 \text{ MBq}}$$

Approximately **37%** of the activity has decayed in 4 hours (since 4 h < 6.02 h < 8 h).

---

## 3.5 Inverse Square Law — Source-to-Surface Distance (SSD) Correction

**Problem:** A Cobalt-60 unit delivers a dose rate of **1.50 Gy/min at SSD = 80 cm**. What is the dose rate at **SSD = 100 cm**?

**Step 1 — Apply the inverse square law**
$$\dot{D_2} = \dot{D_1} \left(\frac{SSD_1}{SSD_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**
$$\dot{D_2} = 1.50 \times \left(\frac{80}{100}\right)^2 = 1.50 \times 0.64 = 0.96$$

$$\boxed{\dot{D_2} = 0.96 \text{ Gy/min}}$$

---

| Property | MRI | SPECT | PET | Cobalt-60 Therapy | LINAC (6 MV) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy** | RF (63.86 MHz @ 1.5T) | 140 keV (Tc-99m) | 511 keV | 1.25 MeV γ | 6 MV X-ray |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Larmor frequency and state the Larmor equation. Calculate the Larmor frequency for a proton in a 1.5 T MRI scanner.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Larmor Frequency:** The precession frequency of a nuclear magnetic moment (e.g., a hydrogen proton) about an applied static magnetic field B₀. It is the resonant frequency at which RF energy is absorbed by the nuclei to flip their spins.

> **Larmor Equation:** $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ where γ is the gyromagnetic ratio and B₀ is the static field strength.

> **Calculation:** For hydrogen, γ = 42.58 MHz/T.
> $$f_0 = 42.58 \times 1.5 = 63.87 \text{ MHz}$$

> **Answer:** $f_0 = 63.87$ MHz.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is meant by T1 and T2 relaxation in MRI? Mention their typical ranges in biological tissues.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **T1 (Longitudinal / Spin-Lattice Relaxation):** The time constant for the recovery of longitudinal magnetization Mz along B₀, involving energy transfer from excited protons to the surrounding molecular lattice. T1 ranges from ~250 ms (fat) to > 4000 ms (CSF).

> **T2 (Transverse / Spin-Spin Relaxation):** The time constant for the decay of transverse magnetization Mxy due to dephasing of proton spins from local field inhomogeneities. T2 ranges from ~50 ms (liver/muscle) to > 2000 ms (CSF).

> Short T1 → bright on T1-weighted (fat). Long T2 → bright on T2-weighted (fluid).

---

### Question 3: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the four major components of a gamma camera and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Collimator** | Lead plate with parallel holes that restricts incoming γ-rays to those traveling perpendicular to the detector face, providing spatial resolution. |
| 2 | **Scintillation Crystal (NaI:Tl)** | Converts incident gamma photons into visible light scintillations. |
| 3 | **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) Array** | Converts scintillation light into amplified electrical pulses with high gain (~10⁶). |
| 4 | **Position Computer + PHA** | Computes the (X, Y) centroid of the light pulse (Anger logic) and analyzes pulse height to select only the photopeak energy. |

---

### Question 4: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between SPECT and PET imaging systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SPECT | PET |
|---|---|---|
| **Radiotracer** | Single γ emitter (e.g., Tc-99m, 140 keV) | β⁺ emitter (e.g., F-18 FDG); produces 511 keV annihilation photons |
| **Detection** | Single photon + physical collimator | Coincidence detection of two 180° photons (electronic collimation) |
| **Resolution** | 8–10 mm | 4–6 mm |
| **Sensitivity** | Lower | Higher (~10×) |
| **Quantification** | Qualitative (limited SUV) | Quantitative (SUV possible) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (cyclotron often needed) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **principle and operation of an MRI system**. Explain the role of the static magnetic field, gradient coils, and RF coils.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **Larmor equation** and the **MRI spin-echo signal equation**. A 3 T MRI scanner is used to image a patient. Calculate the **Larmor wavelength** in free space.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) MRI Principle and Operation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** MRI uses Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) of hydrogen protons (¹H) in tissue. When placed in a strong static magnetic field B₀, the protons' magnetic moments align with B₀. An RF pulse at the Larmor frequency tips the magnetization into the transverse plane. As protons relax (T1, T2), they emit RF signals that are spatially encoded by gradient coils and reconstructed into tomographic images.

**Major Subsystems (4 marks):**

1. **Superconducting Magnet (1 mark):** Generates a homogeneous, stable static field B₀ (0.5–3 T clinical; up to 7 T research). The magnet is wound from Nb-Ti or Nb₃Sn wire, cooled by liquid helium to ~4 K. Field homogeneity is critical (≤ few ppm over imaging volume).

2. **Gradient Coils (1 mark):** Three orthogonal coils (X, Y, Z) produce linear magnetic field variations across the bore. They are used for:
   - **Slice selection (Gss):** Selects a 2D slice during RF excitation (along, say, z).
   - **Frequency encoding (Gfe):** Spatially tags MR signal frequency along x.
   - **Phase encoding (Gpe):** Spatially tags MR signal phase along y, applied in N steps.
   - Gradient strength: typically 30–40 mT/m; slew rate 100–200 T/m/s.

3. **RF Coils (1 mark):** Transmit RF pulses at the Larmor frequency; receive the weak NMR signal (μV level). Body coil (transmit) and surface phased-array coils (receive) are used. Coils are tuned and matched to Larmor frequency.

4. **Computer & Pulse Sequencer (1 mark):** Controls timing (TR, TE, flip angle), image reconstruction (inverse FT), and display.

**Operation Sequence (2 marks):**
- Step 1: Patient in bore → proton alignment.
- Step 2: 90° RF pulse → M tipped into xy plane.
- Step 3: Gradients + 180° refocusing pulse → echoes generated.
- Step 4: Signal sampled in k-space → 2D/3D inverse FT → image.

**Block Diagram (0 mark — textual):** Refer Figure 4.1 from Section 4.

---

**(b) Larmor Equation & Spin-Echo Signal (7 marks):**

**Derivation of Larmor Equation (3 marks):**

The torque on a magnetic moment in a magnetic field:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

Newton's second law for angular momentum:
$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \frac{1}{\gamma}\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt}$$

Equating:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \gamma (\vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0})$$

With $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$, this gives $\mu_x, \mu_y$ oscillating in the xy-plane and $\mu_z$ constant. The solution is precession at:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

For hydrogen, $\gamma/(2\pi) = 42.58$ MHz/T.

**Derivation of Spin-Echo Signal (3 marks):**

In spin-echo, the 90° pulse tips M₀ → xy plane. Mz recovers during TR:
$$M_z(TR) = M_0 (1 - e^{-TR/T_1})$$

After 180° refocusing, Mxy at echo time TE:
$$M_{xy}(TE) = M_0 (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) e^{-TE/T_2}$$

Including proton density:
$$S = k \cdot \rho \cdot (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) e^{-TE/T_2}$$

**Larmor Wavelength Calculation (1 mark):**

At 3 T: $f_0 = 42.58 \times 3 = 127.74$ MHz
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_0} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{127.74 \times 10^6} = 2.35 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 2.35 \text{ m}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper] — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **principle of a gamma camera (Anger camera)** with a block diagram. List its main components and the role of the **collimator** and **pulse height analyzer (PHA)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **principle of a Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** for external beam radiotherapy. A Cobalt-60 source delivers **1.50 Gy/min at 80 cm SSD**. Calculate the dose rate at **100 cm SSD**. State the inverse square law.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Gamma Camera (Anger Camera) (7 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** A gamma camera detects gamma photons emitted by a radiopharmaceutical inside the patient. The **collimator** restricts photons to those traveling perpendicular to the detector. The **NaI(Tl) scintillation crystal** converts each gamma photon into a flash of visible light. The **PMT array** converts this light into proportional electrical pulses. A **position computer** calculates the (X, Y) centroid of the light distribution (Anger positioning), and a **Pulse Height Analyzer (PHA)** selects only those pulses corresponding to the photopeak energy (e.g., 140 ± 14 keV for Tc-99m), rejecting scattered photons.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer Figure 4.2 from Section 4.

**Main Components (2 marks):**
1. Collimator (Pb, ~25 mm thick, hexagonal holes ~1.5 mm)
2. NaI(Tl) crystal (~9.5 mm thick, 50 cm diameter)
3. Light guide
4. PMT array (37 / 61 / 91 tubes)
5. Position computer (X, Y, Z)
6. Pulse height analyzer (energy window)
7. Display and computer

**Role of Collimator (1 mark):** Acts as a "lens" in reverse. Only photons traveling parallel to the holes reach the crystal. This defines the spatial origin of the detected gamma ray and provides spatial resolution (~8–10 mm). Collimators are heavy (~50 kg) and reduce sensitivity (~10⁻⁴).

**Role of PHA (1 mark):** Discriminates against Compton-scattered photons that would otherwise degrade image contrast. Accepts only pulses within a 15–20% energy window centered on the photopeak (e.g., 126–154 keV for Tc-99m).

---

**(b) LINAC Principle and Calculation (7 marks):**

**Principle (3 marks):** A **Linear Accelerator (LINAC)** accelerates electrons to high energies (6–25 MeV) using high-power RF (S-band, 2.856 GHz) in a waveguide structure, then either:
- (i) Strikes a high-Z target (W, Pt) to produce **bremsstrahlung X-ray beams** for photon therapy, OR
- (ii) Uses **scattering foils** to spread the electron beam for **electron therapy**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer Figure 4.4 from Section 4.

**Main Subsystems (2 marks):**
1. **Electron Gun** — thermionic emission of electrons.
2. **RF Source** — Magnetron (≤6 MV) or Klystron (≥6 MV) at 2.856 GHz.
3. **Accelerating Waveguide** — travelling-wave (low energy) or standing-wave (clinical).
4. **Bending Magnet** — 270° achromatic bend for isocentric mounting.
5. **Treatment Head** — Target, flattening filter, monitor chambers, MLC.
6. **MLC** — shapes the beam conformally to the PTV.

**Inverse Square Law (1 mark):** The intensity of radiation from a point source is inversely proportional to the square of the distance from the source:
$$I \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$

**Calculation (0 mark in exam; full 1 mark for the answer):**

Given: $\dot{D_1} = 1.50$ Gy/min at $SSD_1 = 80$ cm. Find $\dot{D_2}$ at $SSD_2 = 100$ cm.
$$\dot{D_2} = \dot{D_1} \left(\frac{SSD_1}{SSD_2}\right)^2 = 1.50 \times \left(\frac{80}{100}\right)^2 = 1.50 \times 0.64$$

$$\boxed{\dot{D_2} = 0.96 \text{ Gy/min}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Larmor Equation Mistakes (Lost 2–3 marks):**
>    - Writing $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ when the question asks for f₀. **Fix:** $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ for f₀ in Hz/MHz, $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ for angular frequency in rad/s.
>    - Confusing **Tesla** and **Gauss** (1 T = 10,000 G).
>    - Forgetting the **2π factor** when converting between angular and cyclic frequency.
>
> 2. **Decay Equation Mistakes (Lost 2 marks):**
>    - Mixing up $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ (decreasing) with $A = A_0 e^{+\lambda t}$ (growing). **Remember:** Activity DECREASES with time.
>    - Forgetting to convert $t_{1/2}$ to the **same time units** as t.
>
> 3. **MRI Block Diagram Mistakes (Lost 2–3 marks):**
>    - Skipping the **gradient coils** (a critical subsystem). Many students draw only magnet + RF.
>    - Forgetting to mention **T1 and T2 relaxation** mechanism in the description.
>    - Not labeling the Larmor frequency or flip angle.
>
> 4. **Gamma Camera Mistakes (Lost 2 marks):**
>    - Drawing the collimator as a single line — must show it as a **multi-hole Pb plate**.
>    - Forgetting the **Anger positioning logic** (centroid calculation from PMT signals).
>    - Not mentioning NaI(Tl) crystal specifically (other scintillators are accepted but NaI:Tl is the standard).
>
> 5. **LINAC Mistakes (Lost 2 marks):**
>    - Forgetting to mention the **bending magnet** for isocentric geometry.
>    - Confusing the role of **magnetron** (lower energies) vs **klystron** (higher energies).
>    - Forgetting the **MLC** (Multi-Leaf Collimator) which is the most important beam-shaping device in modern LINACs.
>
> 6. **Unit Conversion Errors:**
>    - 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq — students often write 3.7 × 10⁹ (off by 10×).
>    - 1 Gy = 100 rad (old unit).
>    - 1 Sv = 100 rem (old unit).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (MRI + Nuclear Medicine + Radiation Therapy)**
>
> **MRI Fundamentals:**
> - MRI is **non-ionizing** (no X-rays, no radiation dose).
> - Larmor equation: $f_0 = \gamma B_0 = 42.58 \times B_0$ MHz for ¹H.
> - At 1.5 T → **63.86 MHz**; at 3 T → **127.74 MHz**; at 7 T → **298 MHz**.
> - **T1** = longitudinal (spin-lattice) recovery; **T2** = transverse (spin-spin) decay.
> - Three gradient coils → slice-select, frequency-encode, phase-encode.
> - **Spin-Echo:** $S \propto \rho (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) e^{-TE/T_2}$.
> - Pulse sequences: SE, GRE, IR (FLAIR, STIR), EPI.
> - Subsystems: Superconducting magnet, Gradient coils, RF coils, Computer/Cryocooler.
> - Safety: **No ferromagnetic implants** (projectile risk), avoid in patients with pacemakers.
>
> **Nuclear Medicine:**
> - **Gamma camera (Anger):** Collimator → NaI(Tl) → PMT array → Position computer → PHA.
> - **Tc-99m:** 140 keV γ, t½ = 6.02 h (generator: Mo-99 → Tc-99m).
> - **F-18 FDG:** β⁺ emitter → 511 keV annihilation photons, t½ = 110 min (cyclotron-produced).
> - **SPECT:** rotating gamma camera, single photon, 8–10 mm resolution.
> - **PET:** coincidence detection, no collimator, 4–6 mm resolution, SUV quantification.
> - **Decay equation:** $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, $t_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$.
> - 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq.
>
> **Radiation Therapy:**
> - **Teletherapy:** source external to patient. Cobalt-60 (1.25 MeV γ) or LINAC (6–25 MV X).
> - **Brachytherapy:** source placed inside or adjacent to tumor (LDR/HDR).
> - **LINAC components:** Electron gun → Klystron/Magnetron (2.856 GHz) → Waveguide → Bending magnet → Target (X-rays) or foils (electrons) → Flattening filter → MLC → Patient.
> - **Dose unit:** Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg. **Activity:** Becquerel (Bq).
> - **ICRU Volumes:** GTV (visible tumor) → CTV (microscopic) → PTV (setup margin) → OAR.
> - **Inverse square law:** $I \propto 1/d^2$ — used for SSD corrections.
> - **Bragg peak:** Protons/ions deposit maximum energy at end of range; useful for sparing distal tissue.
> - **OER** (Oxygen Enhancement Ratio) ~ 2.5–3 — hypoxic tumors are radio-resistant.
>
> **Cross-Cutting High-Yield Constants:**
> - γ (¹H) = 42.58 MHz/T (gyromagnetic ratio)
> - c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
> - 1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq
> - 511 keV = positron annihilation photon energy
> - 140 keV = Tc-99m gamma
> - 1.25 MeV = Co-60 gamma (average)
> - 2.856 GHz = clinical LINAC RF frequency
> - 1 Gy = 100 rad; 1 Sv = 100 rem

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultrasonic Imaging System (Introduction and Basic Principle)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEBRT611 - BIOMEDICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Ultrasonic Imaging System (Introduction and Basic Principle) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ultrasonic Imaging System (Diagnostic Sonography):** A non-invasive, real-time, radiation-free medical imaging modality that uses high-frequency mechanical sound waves (typically **1 MHz to 20 MHz**, well above the human hearing threshold of **20 kHz**) to visualize the internal structure, morphology, and motion of soft tissues, organs, and blood flow within the human body. The system operates on the **Pulse-Echo Principle** using the **Piezoelectric Effect** for transduction between electrical and acoustic energy. ### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Breakdown | Term | Formal Meaning | Diagnostic Range | |------|----------------|------------------| | **Ultrasound** | Sound waves with frequency > 20 kHz | **1 – 20 MHz** (medical) | | **Acoustic Wave** | Longitudinal mechanical pressure wave | Travels through medium | | **Transducer** | Device converting electrical ↔ acoustic energy | Piezoelectric crystal | | **Pulse-Echo** | Transmission of short bursts and detection of reflections | Fundamental operating principle | | **A-Scan / B-Scan / M-Scan** | Display modes (Amplitude / Brightness / Motion) | Output formats | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bat and the Submarine" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Bats, SONAR, and the Stethoscope of the Modern Era** > > Imagine you are standing in a dark, foggy room and you shout "Hello!". The sound travels, hits the far wall, and the echo returns. By measuring **how long** the echo took to come back, you can estimate **how far** the wall is. If the wall has a soft curtain on it, the echo will be weak; if it has a hard metal sheet, the echo will be loud. This is **exactly** how an ultrasonic scanner works — except instead of audible sound, it uses *ultrasound* (silent to humans), and instead of a single shout, it sends thousands of *pulses* per second, and listens with an extremely sensitive *microphone* called a **piezoelectric transducer**. - The **shout** → High-voltage electrical pulse sent to the transducer. - The **echo** → Reflected sound wave returning from tissue boundaries. - The **dark room** → The inside of the human body, opaque to light but transparent to sound. - The **time delay** → Distance into the body (depth calculation). - The **loudness (amplitude)** of the echo → Acoustic mismatch at the boundary (echogenicity). --- ## 1.3 Why Ultrasound? — The Clinical Motivation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Properties that make ultrasound the workhorse of modern diagnostics:** > 1. **Non-ionizing radiation** → Safe for fetuses, neonates, and repeated examinations. > 2. **Real-time imaging** → Frames acquired at 30–60+ per second; can visualize motion (heart, blood flow). > 3. **Portability** → Compact bedside units (point-of-care). > 4. **Cost-effective** → 5–10× cheaper than CT or MRI. > 5. **No special shielding** required, unlike X-ray rooms. > 6. **Doppler capability** → Measures blood flow velocity. --- ## 1.4 The Average Speed of Ultrasound in Soft Tissue > [!NOTE] > The single most important physical constant in the entire subject: > $$\boxed{c_{\text{soft tissue}} \approx 1540 \text{ m/s}}$$ > > This is the **assumed value** used in ALL diagnostic ultrasound depth/time calculations. Variability between tissues is small (±5%), so 1540 m/s is the **clinical standard**. | Tissue Type | Speed of Sound (m/s) | Acoustic Impedance (kg/m²s × 10⁶) | |-------------|----------------------|-----------------------------------| | Air | 330 | 0.0004 | | Fat | 1450 | 1.38 | | Water | 1480 | 1.48 | | **Soft tissue (avg)** | **1540** | **1.63** | | Kidney | 1560 | 1.62 | | Blood | 1570 | 1.66 | | Muscle | 1580 | 1.70 | | Bone (cortical) | 4080 | 7.80 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Fundamental Physics of the Ultrasonic Wave Ultrasound is a **longitudinal mechanical wave** — particle oscillation is parallel to the direction of wave propagation. This is unlike electromagnetic (X-ray, MRI) waves, which are transverse and require no medium. ### 2.1.1 Wave Equation — The Three Pillars The relationship connecting the three fundamental properties of any wave is: $$v = f \times \lambda$$ Where: - $v$ = velocity of the wave in the medium (m/s) - $f$ = frequency of the wave (Hz) - $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in imaging:** Higher frequency ⇒ shorter wavelength ⇒ **better axial (depth) resolution**, but **shallower penetration**. This is the fundamental trade-off in transducer selection. --- ## 2.2 Acoustic Impedance — The "Resistance" of Tissue to Sound Every tissue opposes the passage of sound to a degree, characterized by a property called **Acoustic Impedance (Z)**. $$Z = \rho \times c$$ Where: - $Z$ = Acoustic impedance (Rayl, or kg/m²·s, often expressed as ×10⁶) - $\rho$ = density of the medium (kg/m³) - $c$ = velocity of sound in the medium (m/s) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustic impedance is the *single most important* concept in ultrasound imaging.** Echoes (reflections) are generated ONLY where there is a change in acoustic impedance between adjacent tissues. No impedance difference = no echo = no image. --- ## 2.3 Reflection and Transmission at Tissue Interfaces When an ultrasound beam strikes an interface between two tissues with impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, part of the energy is **reflected** and part is **transmitted**. ### 2.3.1 Reflection Coefficient (Intensity) $$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$ ### 2.3.2 Transmission Coefficient (Intensity) $$T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note that $R + T = 1$ (energy conservation). Both are dimensionless ratios between 0 and 1. ### 2.3.3 Clinical Interpretation of Reflections | Reflection Magnitude | Tissue Interface Example | Image Appearance | |----------------------|--------------------------|------------------| | Very High (R ≈ 0.99) | Soft tissue ↔ **Bone / Air** | Bright white (hyperechoic) | | Moderate | Soft tissue ↔ **Organ boundary** | Mid-grey | | Low | **Fat–muscle, kidney–liver** | Subtle grey | | None (R ≈ 0) | Within homogeneous tissue | Black (anechoic) | --- ## 2.4 Attenuation — The Loss of Signal with Depth As ultrasound propagates, intensity decreases due to: 1. **Absorption** (conversion to heat — dominant mechanism) 2. **Scattering** (Rayleigh scattering from small structures) 3. **Reflection** (already considered) 4. **Divergence** (beam spreading) $$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha x}$$ Where $\alpha$ is the **attenuation coefficient** (Np/m), and in soft tissue: $$\alpha \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ dB/cm/MHz}$$ Empirically: $$\alpha = \alpha_0 \cdot f^n \quad \text{where } n \approx 1 \text{ (soft tissue)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The famous rule of thumb in clinical ultrasound:** *Attenuation ≈ 1 dB/cm/MHz (one-way).* For a 5 MHz probe, signal loses ~5 dB per cm of depth — this is why time-gain compensation (TGC) is essential in the receiver. --- ## 2.5 The Pulse-Echo Principle — Depth Calculation This is the **operating heart** of every diagnostic ultrasound system. If a pulse is transmitted at time $t = 0$ and the echo returns at time $t = \tau$, then the depth of the reflector is: $$d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$ The factor of 2 accounts for the round-trip travel (down and back up). > [!NOTE] > The assumed value **c = 1540 m/s** is used here. The 1540 m/s is a clever number: it conveniently gives 13.3 μs per cm of round-trip (1 cm depth = 13.3 μs return time). --- ## 2.6 Piezoelectric Effect — The Heart of the Transducer > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pierre and Jacques Curie, 1880** — Discovered that certain crystals (quartz, Rochelle salt, tourmaline) develop an electric charge when mechanically stressed, and conversely deform when an electric field is applied. | Effect | Phenomenon | Use in Ultrasound | |--------|------------|---------------------| | **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** | Mechanical stress → Electric voltage | **Receiving** mode (echo → voltage) | | **Inverse Piezoelectric Effect** | Electric voltage → Mechanical deformation | **Transmitting** mode (voltage → pulse) | **Modern transducer materials:** - **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate):** Most common, high coupling coefficient. - **PVDF (Polyvinylidene Fluoride):** Polymer, broadband, used in high-frequency probes. - **Composite materials:** PZT + epoxy, for better matching and lower impedance. --- ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All the formulas you must memorize for the KTU 2024 examination on this topic.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $v = f \lambda$ | Wave equation | m/s, Hz, m | | 2 | $Z = \rho c$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/m²·s (Rayl) | | 3 | $R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ | Intensity reflection coefficient | dimensionless | | 4 | $T = \frac{4 Z_1 Z_2}{(Z_1 + Z_2)^2}$ | Intensity transmission coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$ | Exponential attenuation (Np) | W/m² | | 6 | $\text{Attenuation (dB)} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot x$ | dB loss (clinical use) | dB | | 7 | $d = \frac{c \tau}{2}$ | Depth from round-trip time | m | | 8 | $\text{PRF}_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 D_{max}}$ | Maximum pulse repetition frequency | Hz | | 9 | $\text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}$ | Axial resolution (pulse length-limited) | m | | 10 | $\text{Lateral Resolution} \approx \lambda \cdot f_{\#}$ | Lateral resolution (diffraction-limited) | m | | 11 | $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho c A^2 \omega^2$ | Acoustic intensity related to particle velocity | W/m² | > Where: $\Delta f$ = bandwidth of transducer, $f_{\#}$ = f-number of the focused beam, $D_{max}$ = maximum depth of interest, $A$ = particle displacement amplitude, $\omega$ = angular frequency. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Deriving the Depth Equation from the Pulse-Echo Principle **Problem Statement:** A piezoelectric transducer emits a 3.5 MHz pulse at $t = 0$. The echo from a deep tissue interface returns to the transducer at $t = 52 \, \mu s$. Calculate the depth of the interface. Assume $c = 1540$ m/s. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Identify the physical situation:** The pulse travels from the transducer down to the reflector and back to the transducer. Total distance covered by the wave = $2d$, where $d$ is the one-way depth. **Step 2 — Apply the basic kinematic relationship:** Distance equals velocity multiplied by time: $$2d = c \cdot \tau$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for depth $d$:** $$d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$d = \frac{(1540 \text{ m/s}) \times (52 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Compute the numerator:** $$1540 \times 52 \times 10^{-6} = 80080 \times 10^{-6} = 0.08008 \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Divide by 2:** $$d = \frac{0.08008}{2} = 0.04004 \text{ m} \approx 4.0 \text{ cm}$$ **Step 7 — Final Answer:** $$\boxed{d \approx 4 \text{ cm}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reflection at a Tissue Interface (Soft Tissue to Bone) **Problem Statement:** An ultrasound beam traveling in soft tissue ($Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ kg/m²s) strikes a cortical bone surface ($Z_2 = 7.80 \times 10^6$ kg/m²s). Calculate the percentage of intensity reflected and transmitted at the interface. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Write down the reflection coefficient formula:** $$R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ (the $\times 10^6$ cancels in ratio):** $$R = \left( \frac{7.80 - 1.63}{7.80 + 1.63} \right)^2$$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator:** $$7.80 - 1.63 = 6.17$$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator:** $$7.80 + 1.63 = 9.43$$ **Step 5 — Form the ratio:** $$\frac{6.17}{9.43} = 0.6543$$ **Step 6 — Square the ratio (intensity, not pressure):** $$R = (0.6543)^2 = 0.4281$$ **Step 7 — Convert to percentage:** $$R \approx 42.8\%$$ **Step 8 — Calculate transmission coefficient using $T = 1 - R$:** $$T = 1 - 0.4281 = 0.5719 \approx 57.2\%$$ **Step 9 — Clinical Interpretation:** > [!NOTE] > Nearly **43% of the beam is reflected** at the soft tissue-bone interface. This is why bone creates a bright (hyperechoic) line in the image, and why imaging *through* bone (e.g., transcranial ultrasound) is extremely difficult. The remaining 57% continues, but most of that is rapidly absorbed by the bone. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Axial Resolution as a Function of Transducer Bandwidth **Problem Statement:** A typical abdominal transducer has a center frequency $f_0 = 3.5$ MHz and a bandwidth of $\Delta f = 2.5$ MHz. Calculate the axial resolution. (Use $c = 1540$ m/s). ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Understand the concept:** Axial resolution is the minimum separation between two reflectors along the beam axis that can be resolved as separate echoes. It is limited by the spatial pulse length (SPL) of the emitted wave: $$\text{Axial Resolution} = \frac{\text{SPL}}{2} = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}$$ **Step 2 — State the formula clearly:** $$\Delta z = \frac{c}{2 \Delta f}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the given values:** $$\Delta z = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times (2.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz})}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the denominator:** $$2 \times 2.5 \times 10^6 = 5.0 \times 10^6$$ **Step 5 — Perform the division:** $$\Delta z = \frac{1540}{5.0 \times 10^6} = 308 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to mm:** $$\Delta z = 0.308 \text{ mm} \approx 0.31 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 7 — Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\Delta z \approx 0.31 \text{ mm}}$$ **Step 8 — Engineering Insight:** > [!NOTE] > To improve axial resolution, increase bandwidth $\Delta f$. Modern broadband transducers (e.g., 2–5 MHz nominal) achieve sub-millimeter resolution. The trade-off is that wider bandwidth typically also increases noise, requiring better receiver design. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF) **Problem Statement:** For an abdominal scan at a maximum depth of interest $D_{max} = 20$ cm, find the maximum pulse repetition frequency to avoid range ambiguity. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Understand the constraint:** A new pulse cannot be transmitted until the echo from the deepest target of the previous pulse has returned. Otherwise, late echoes from the previous pulse will be misinterpreted as early echoes from the new pulse (range ambiguity). **Step 2 — The round-trip time to maximum depth:** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{2 D_{max}}{c}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum PRF is the inverse of this round-trip time:** $$\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{1}{\tau_{max}} = \frac{c}{2 D_{max}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the values:** $$\text{PRF}_{max} = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{2 \times 0.20 \text{ m}} = \frac{1540}{0.40}$$ **Step 5 — Compute:** $$\text{PRF}_{max} = 3850 \text{ Hz} = 3.85 \text{ kHz}$$ **Step 6 — Final Answer:** $$\boxed{\text{PRF}_{max} \approx 3.85 \text{ kHz}}$$ > [!NOTE] > In practice, designers use a PRF slightly lower than this to provide a "listening gap." For Doppler ultrasound, lower PRFs are needed to avoid aliasing of low-velocity blood flow — another fundamental trade-off in the system. --- ## 3.5 Worked Example — Attenuation in Tissue **Problem Statement:** A 5 MHz transducer sends a pulse into muscle. The intensity at the transducer face is $I_0 = 10$ mW/cm². Calculate the intensity at a depth of 6 cm. Use $\alpha = 1$ dB/cm/MHz. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Calculate total attenuation in dB:** $$\text{Attenuation} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot x = 1 \cdot 5 \cdot 6 = 30 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2 — Convert dB to a linear ratio:** $$\frac{I}{I_0} = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001$$ **Step 3 — Calculate final intensity:** $$I = 0.001 \times 10 \text{ mW/cm}^2 = 0.01 \text{ mW/cm}^2$$ **Step 4 — Final Answer:** $$\boxed{I(6 \text{ cm}) = 0.01 \text{ mW/cm}^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > A 30 dB loss means the signal is reduced by a factor of 1000 in just 6 cm. The TGC (Time-Gain Compensation) amplifier in the receiver applies progressively higher gain to echoes returning from greater depths, equalizing the image brightness. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Ultrasonic Imaging System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Master Clock
and Timing Controller] --> B[Pulser
Transmitter] A --> C[Time Gain
Compensation TGC] B -- High Voltage Pulse --> D[Piezoelectric
Transducer] D -- Acoustic Pulse --> E[Patient Body
Tissue Interfaces] E -- Reflected Echoes --> D D -- Electrical Echo Signal --> C C --> F[Receiver
Amplifier and Filter] F --> G[Signal Processor
Envelope Detection] G --> H[Scan Converter
Digital Memory] H --> I[Display Unit
Monitor] H --> J[Storage
Memory Archive] I --> K[Clinician
Interpretation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read this block diagram:** The system is **monostatic** — the same transducer transmits and receives. A **T/R switch** (often inside the pulser block) isolates the high-voltage transmit pulse from the sensitive low-voltage receive electronics. The timing controller synchronizes transmit pulses with the scan converter's writing of echo data into the right pixel. --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Pulse-Echo Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant T as Transducer participant TC as Timing Controller participant P as Pulser participant R as Receiver participant SC as Scan Converter participant DSP as Display TC->>P: Trigger pulse at t=0 P->>T: 100-200V excitation pulse T->>T: Piezo crystal vibrates 1-2 cycles T->>T: Acoustic pulse travels into body Note over T: Pulse travels at 1540 m/s T->>T: Echo returns from reflector T->>R: Weak echo voltage (microvolts to millivolts) R->>R: Amplify with TGC R->>R: Envelope detection R->>SC: Store at position f(t) = ct/2 SC->>DSP: Build image line by line ``` --- ## 4.3 Display Modes Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB Root[Ultrasound Display Modes] Root --> A[A-Mode Amplitude] Root --> B[B-Mode Brightness] Root --> M[M-Mode Motion] Root --> D[Doppler Mode] Root --> CFM[Color Flow Mode] A --> A1[1D Spikes on Time Axis] A --> A2[Used in Ophthalmology] B --> B1[2D Greyscale Image] B --> B2[Real-time scanning] B --> B3[Most common mode] M --> M1[1D vs Time] M --> M2[Cardiac Valve Motion] D --> D1[Velocity Spectrum] D --> D2[Blood Flow Measurement] CFM --> CFM1[Color Overlay on B-mode] CFM1 --> CFM2[Red Away Blue Toward] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** A-mode and M-mode are 1-D presentations; B-mode is the 2-D cross-sectional image most people associate with "ultrasound." A **real-time B-mode scanner** uses phased-array or linear-array transducers to sweep the beam across a 2D plane at frame rates of 30–60 fps. --- ## 4.4 Transducer Construction Cross-Section (Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Transducer["PIEZOELECTRIC TRANSDUCER ASSEMBLY"] direction TB H[Housing and Electrical Connector] H --> M[Matching Layer 1-4 quarter wavelength impedance matching] M --> P[Active Piezoelectric Element PZT Disc] P --> B[Backing Damping Material High acoustic impedance to shorten pulse] B --> E[Backing Epoxy] end P -. Generates ultrasound .-> Body[Patient Body via Coupling Gel] Body -. Reflects echoes back .-> P ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why a matching layer?** The piezo element has very high Z (≈ 30 MRayl) but tissue has Z ≈ 1.6 MRayl. Without matching, >99% of energy is reflected back into the crystal. A ¼-wavelength matching layer of intermediate Z (≈ 5–10 MRayl) maximizes transmission. **Why backing material?** It absorbs backward-travelling vibrations, shortening the pulse (improving axial resolution) at the cost of sensitivity. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** These are direct, definition-based questions worth 3 marks each. Expect a 2–3 sentence crisp answer. ### Question 1: Define acoustic impedance. Mention its SI unit and typical values for soft tissue and bone. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** Acoustic impedance is the product of the density of a medium and the velocity of sound in that medium, $Z = \rho c$. It is a measure of the resistance offered by the medium to the propagation of sound waves. The SI unit is the Rayl (kg/m²·s), often expressed in MRayl (×10⁶) for biological tissue. - **Soft tissue (avg):** $Z \approx 1.63 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s = 1.63 MRayl - **Cortical bone:** $Z \approx 7.80 \times 10^6$ kg/m²·s = 7.80 MRayl Acoustic impedance is the key parameter that determines the magnitude of echo reflection at a tissue interface. --- ### Question 2: What is the piezoelectric effect? Why is it important in ultrasound transducers? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The piezoelectric effect is the ability of certain crystalline materials (e.g., quartz, PZT) to convert mechanical stress into an electric charge (**direct effect**) and, conversely, to deform mechanically when an electric voltage is applied (**inverse effect**). **Importance in ultrasound:** 1. **Transmission:** Applying a high-voltage pulse to the crystal causes it to vibrate, generating the ultrasound wave. 2. **Reception:** Returning echoes mechanically deform the crystal, producing a measurable voltage that represents the echo. 3. The same crystal can thus act as both transmitter and receiver, enabling the pulse-echo technique. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as 7+7 (sub-parts a and b). The student chooses ONE of the two alternatives. Model answer key with marking distribution is provided. --- ### Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a diagnostic ultrasonic imaging system. List and briefly explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ultrasound pulse is sent into soft tissue. It returns after $78 \, \mu s$. Calculate the depth of the reflecting surface. If the same pulse is sent at 4 MHz, calculate the wavelength of the ultrasound in soft tissue. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Solution to (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks):** **[Block Diagram — 3 marks]** ``` Master Clock → Pulser → T/R Switch → Transducer ⇄ Patient ↘ Receiver → TGC → Envelope Detector ↘ Scan Converter → Display ``` **Operation (4 marks):** 1. **Master Clock / Timing Controller (0.5 mark):** Generates the synchronization pulses that determine PRF and coordinate transmit/receive cycles. Typically runs at 1–10 kHz. 2. **Pulser (Transmitter) (0.5 mark):** Produces a brief high-voltage spike (50–200 V) lasting ~1 μs to drive the piezo crystal. 3. **T/R Switch (0.5 mark):** Protects the sensitive receiver from the high-voltage transmit pulse using diodes or relays. 4. **Transducer (1 mark):** Piezoelectric crystal converts electrical pulse to acoustic wave (transmit) and acoustic echo back to voltage (receive). Also focuses the beam. 5. **Receiver with TGC (1 mark):** Amplifies the weak echo with depth-dependent gain (Time Gain Compensation) to compensate for attenuation. Typical gain: 60–100 dB. 6. **Signal Processor (0.5 mark):** Performs envelope detection (rectification + low-pass filtering) to extract the demodulated signal. 7. **Scan Converter and Display (1 mark):** Stores echo data in digital memory, maps to 2D image, and displays at 30–60 fps. --- **Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** - Round-trip time $\tau = 78 \, \mu s = 78 \times 10^{-6}$ s - Frequency $f = 4$ MHz = $4 \times 10^6$ Hz - Speed of sound in soft tissue $c = 1540$ m/s **Part (i): Depth calculation (3.5 marks)** Using the depth formula: $$d = \frac{c \cdot \tau}{2}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute (1 mark):** $$d = \frac{1540 \times 78 \times 10^{-6}}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Compute numerator (1 mark):** $$1540 \times 78 \times 10^{-6} = 120120 \times 10^{-6} = 0.12012 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Divide by 2 (0.5 mark):** $$d = 0.06006 \text{ m} \approx 6.0 \text{ cm}$$ $$\boxed{d \approx 6 \text{ cm}}$$ **Part (ii): Wavelength calculation (3.5 marks)** Using $v = f \lambda$: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute (1 mark):** $$\lambda = \frac{1540}{4 \times 10^6}$$ **Step 2 — Compute (1 mark):** $$\lambda = 385 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.385 \text{ mm}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda \approx 0.385 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes Students Make:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $d = c\tau/2$ → answers off by a factor of 2 (full 0 marks even with correct math). > 2. **Using $c = 1500$ or $c = 340$ m/s** → Always use $c = 1540$ m/s for soft tissue in KTU problems unless explicitly told otherwise. $c = 340$ m/s is the speed of sound in **air** — a common confusion. > 3. **Mixing up reflection vs transmission coefficients** → Remember the formula for **intensity** (not pressure) has the SQUARE; pressure reflection uses a non-squared ratio. > 4. **Unit errors** → Convert $\mu s$ to $s$ and MHz to Hz before plugging in. --- ### Question B: (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]` **(a)** Define acoustic impedance. Derive the expression for the intensity reflection coefficient at the interface between two media. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ultrasound beam is incident from soft tissue ($Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl) onto the surface of a kidney ($Z_2 = 1.62 \times 10^6$ Rayl). Calculate (i) the reflection coefficient and (ii) the percentage of intensity reflected. Comment on why such interfaces are difficult to visualize in a B-scan. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Solution to (a) — Theory (7 marks):** **Definition of acoustic impedance (2 marks):** Acoustic impedance $Z$ of a medium is the product of its density $\rho$ and the speed of sound $c$ in that medium: $$Z = \rho \times c$$ It represents the opposition a medium offers to the transmission of sound energy, analogous to electrical impedance. **Derivation of the reflection coefficient (5 marks):** Consider a plane acoustic wave traveling in medium 1 (impedance $Z_1$) striking the boundary with medium 2 (impedance $Z_2$) at normal incidence. Let the incident pressure amplitude be $P_i$, the reflected be $P_r$, and the transmitted be $P_t$. **Step 1 — Apply continuity of pressure at the boundary (1 mark):** $$P_i + P_r = P_t$$ **Step 2 — Apply continuity of particle velocity (1 mark):** $$\frac{P_i}{Z_1} - \frac{P_r}{Z_1} = \frac{P_t}{Z_2}$$ **Step 3 — Solve the two equations for $P_r / P_i$ (2 marks):** After algebraic elimination: $$\frac{P_r}{P_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$ **Step 4 — Convert to intensity ratio using $I \propto P^2$ (1 mark):** $$R = \frac{I_r}{I_i} = \left( \frac{P_r}{P_i} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$$ $$\boxed{R = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2}$$ --- **Solution to (b) — Numerical (7 marks):** **Given:** - $Z_1 = 1.63 \times 10^6$ Rayl (soft tissue) - $Z_2 = 1.62 \times 10^6$ Rayl (kidney) **Part (i): Reflection coefficient (3.5 marks)** $$R = \left( \frac{1.62 - 1.63}{1.62 + 1.63} \right)^2$$ **Step 1 — Numerator (0.5 mark):** $1.62 - 1.63 = -0.01$ **Step 2 — Denominator (0.5 mark):** $1.62 + 1.63 = 3.25$ **Step 3 — Ratio (1 mark):** $-0.01 / 3.25 = -0.003077$ **Step 4 — Square (1 mark):** $(-0.003077)^2 = 9.47 \times 10^{-6}$ **Step 5 — Final (0.5 mark):** $$\boxed{R \approx 9.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$ **Part (ii): Percentage reflected (1.5 marks)** $$R\% = 9.47 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 9.47 \times 10^{-4} \% \approx 0.00095\%$$ **Comment on B-scan visualization (2 marks):** Less than 1 part in 100,000 of the incident intensity is reflected. The echo is so weak that it falls **below the noise floor of the receiver**, making the soft tissue–kidney boundary **essentially invisible** in a B-scan. The kidney parenchyma blends with surrounding soft tissue (no contrast). This is why ultrasound contrast agents (microbubbles) or alternative modalities (CT, MRI) are sometimes used to image such low-contrast organs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Students commonly lose marks here by:** > 1. **Forgetting to square the ratio** (writing $R = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ instead of the squared version).
